title: Missing error propagation
message: The Go Flush returns error and propagates failures from both flushToRemove and updateBatch.Flush; the Rust signature returns (Vec<db::FileInfo>, Vec<String>) with no Result wrapper, so all error paths from both sub-operations are silently dropped. This eliminates the short-circuit behavior where a flushToRemove error prevents updateBatch.Flush from executing.

title: Side-effect persistence replaced with data extraction
message: In Go, updateBatch.Flush() is itself a persistence call that writes accumulated file updates to the database as a side effect. The Rust implementation replaces this with a std::mem::swap that merely drains the Vec out of the struct and returns it to the caller, deferring all persistence responsibility. The method no longer performs a flush; it performs an extract, fundamentally changing the call-site contract and making correctness dependent on every caller remembering to persist the returned data.

title: Execution order reversed
message: Go calls flushToRemove first (line 613), then updateBatch.Flush (line 616). Rust drains updateBatch first (line 280), then calls flushToRemove (line 281). If flushToRemove mutates state that updateBatch.Flush depends on (or vice versa), the reversed order produces different observable behavior. The Go ordering also ensures removes are committed before updates, which may be semantically significant for conflict resolution.

title: Return type *Vector vs i64
message: The Go method returns `*Vector`, a protobuf vector-clock struct containing a slice of Counter entries (ID + Value pairs), which is essential for version-vector comparison and conflict resolution on the wire. The Rust method returns a bare `i64`, collapsing the entire vector clock into a single scalar. This breaks wire/schema compatibility (the protobuf field is message-typed, not int64), eliminates multi-device version tracking semantics, and will produce incorrect results in any conflict-resolution or index-sequence-behavior scenario that compares versions from different devices.

title: Nil/None path lost for absent version
message: The Go method can return `nil` both when the receiver is nil and when `x.Version` is an unset field (nil pointer). The Rust method always returns an `i64` value (defaulting to 0), making it impossible for callers to distinguish "version not set" from "version is zero". This matters for protocol-state-transition and crash-recovery scenarios where an absent version vector signals an uninitialized or reset state distinct from a zero-valued version.

title: Receiver type mismatch vs mapping metadata
message: The mapping metadata declares the Go symbol as `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.GetVersion`, but the provided Go source shows the method on `*FileInfo`. If the Rust implementation is intended to port `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.GetVersion` (which returns `int32` per the protobuf schema) rather than `FileInfo.GetVersion` (which returns `*Vector`), the return type `i64` is still wrong (should be `i32`), and the source-of-truth method is misidentified, risking silent behavioral divergence.

title: Missing duration parameter
message: The Go method accepts a `next time.Duration` parameter that is sent on the `scanDelay` channel to communicate a specific delay to the scan scheduling loop. The Rust method takes no parameter, so callers cannot specify a delay duration, and any call site that needs to pass a non-default delay will silently lose that value.

title: Wrong side-effect semantics
message: The Go method sends the duration on channel `f.scanDelay`, which is consumed by the folder's scan loop to reschedule the next scan after the given delay. The Rust method instead sets `self.scanScheduled = false`, which merely clears a boolean flag. These are not equivalent: Go delays the scan by a caller-specified duration while Rust cancels any pending scheduled scan outright, producing different scan timing behavior under every invocation.

title: Missing done/shutdown guard
message: The Go method uses a `select` with `case <-f.done` so that the operation returns immediately without blocking if the folder has been stopped. The Rust method has no corresponding check against a shutdown or cancellation signal, meaning it will unconditionally mutate state even after the folder should be considered closed, risking state corruption during teardown.

title: Return type mismatch breaks reflection API
message: Go returns `protoreflect.EnumDescriptor`, a rich interface carrying full protobuf schema metadata (FullName, Values, Options, parent FileDescriptor, etc.), while Rust returns `&'static str`. Any consumer that calls chained descriptor methods (e.g. `.FullName()`, `.Values()`, `.Options()`) will fail at compile time in Rust, and any runtime logic that inspects the descriptor for enum value resolution, proto registration, or reflection-based serialization will silently produce wrong results or panic. This is not a cosmetic difference; it breaks wire-level schema introspection required by the protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior test scenarios.

title: Visibility downgrade from public to crate-internal
message: Go `Descriptor()` is an exported method (uppercase) implementing the `protoreflect.EnumDescriptor` interface and callable by any external package. Rust declares it `pub(crate)`, restricting access to the defining crate only. External crates depending on bep-compat that need to obtain the descriptor (e.g. for proto registration or reflection) will not be able to call this method, breaking cross-crate interoperability that the Go API guarantees.

title: Receiver semantics differ subtly
message: Go uses a value receiver `(MessageType)` with no reference to the receiver value — it unconditionally returns `file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes[0].Descriptor()`, a singleton looked up from a package-level generated slice. Rust takes `&self` and calls `descriptor($name)` where `$name` is a macro-expanded compile-time token. While neither actually inspects the instance value, the Go version is tied to a specific index (`[0]`) in a generated type registry, meaning the descriptor identity is coupled to proto codegen ordering. The Rust version uses a name-based lookup, which could diverge if the registry ordering or naming convention drifts between the Go and Rust codegen outputs.

title: Return type is raw i32 instead of enum
message: The Go method returns the strongly-typed enum `FileDownloadProgressUpdateType`, while the Rust method returns a bare `i32`. This loses type-level discrimination and means callers cannot match on named variants; it also risks accepting out-of-range values that the Go enum type would surface during protobuf validation or `String()`/`Enum()` calls, degrading wire-schema fidelity.

title: Missing nil-receiver default value path
message: The Go getter returns the default enum value `FILE_DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS_UPDATE_TYPE_APPEND` (protobuf field-default / zero value 0) when the receiver is nil. The Rust port takes `&self`, so a nil-receiver call is impossible at the language level; however, the port does not document or enforce that a default-constructed `FileDownloadProgressUpdate` initialises `UpdateType` to the equivalent zero-value constant. If the Rust struct is ever constructed with an uninitialised or non-zero default for `UpdateType` (e.g. via `Default` derive that uses a different sentinel), the observable default-path behavior will diverge from Go.

title: FlagLocalReceiveOnly incorrectly cleared
message: Go explicitly preserves FlagLocalReceiveOnly on deleted items (lines 82-84 comment: "Purposely not removing FlagLocalReceiveOnly as the deleted item should still not be sent in index updates"). Rust does the opposite at line 2043, unconditionally clearing the flag via `local.local_flags &= !db::FLAG_LOCAL_RECEIVE_ONLY`. This inverts the intended index-update suppression behavior and will cause reverted items to be incorrectly advertised to peers.

title: Entirely different revert algorithm — spurious global lookup and merge
message: Go never looks up the global file entry; it simply marks every qualifying local file as deleted with a zeroed version vector, relying on a subsequent pull to restore the correct global state. Rust introduces an unrelated algorithm that queries the global file (line 2044), conditionally merges it into the update set (lines 2050-2055), and only marks the local as deleted when the global also has the receive-only flag. This changes which records are persisted and what their content is, breaking the "delete-then-pull" revert contract.

title: No filesystem deletion of unexpected items
message: Go removes non-directory files from the filesystem via `inWritableDir(f.mtimefs.Remove, fi.Name)` (Go line 72) before marking them deleted in the database. Rust performs no filesystem I/O at all, so reverted files remain on disk even though the database considers them deleted, causing persistent inconsistency between disk and DB state.

title: Version vector not zeroed
message: Go explicitly sets `fi.Version = protocol.Vector{}` (Go line 80) so that the subsequent pull will treat the file as needing the global version. Rust never zeroes the version vector, so the puller may consider the local version sufficient and skip re-downloading the correct content.

title: Deleted files not filtered out
message: Go skips files that are already deleted (`fi.IsDeleted()` at Go line 63). Rust only checks for the FLAG_LOCAL_RECEIVE_ONLY bit and does not skip deleted files, so already-deleted entries will be redundantly processed and potentially re-persisted with a new sequence number, corrupting sequence ordering expectations.

title: No directory collection or revertHandleDirs call
message: Go separates directories from files, collecting directory names (Go lines 67-70) and passing them to `revertHandleDirs` (Go line 88) for depth-ordered removal. Rust has no directory-specific handling; directories are processed identically to files, meaning directory entries on disk are never removed and the depth-first deletion order required to successfully remove nested directory trees is absent.

title: Missing FolderScanning/FolderIdle state transitions
message: Go sets the folder state to FolderScanning before iterating and defers FolderIdle (Go lines 46-47), which is observable by the UI and other subsystems that gate operations on folder state. Rust performs no state transitions, so concurrent operations and UI status reporting will behave incorrectly during a revert.

title: SetDeleted semantics differ — missing shortID
message: Go calls `fi.SetDeleted(f.shortID)` (Go line 76) which stamps the file's modified-by field with the local device short ID. Rust sets `local.deleted = true` without recording a modifier identity, which may cause downstream version-vector or conflict-resolution logic to behave differently when comparing deletion origins.

title: Return type loses protobuf reflection capability
message: Go returns `protoreflect.Message`, a rich interface providing descriptor access, dynamic field enumeration, and schema-aware operations used throughout the protobuf runtime for marshaling, merging, and protocol negotiation. The Rust implementation returns a generic `Value` (likely serde-based), which discards all protobuf descriptor metadata and reflection capabilities. Any downstream code relying on `protoreflect.Message` methods such as `Descriptor()`, `Range()`, `Get()`, `NewField()`, or `WhichOneof()` will have no equivalent path in the Rust port, breaking protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior scenarios that inspect or manipulate messages reflectively.

title: Nil receiver path absent
message: Go lines 462-469 explicitly handle a nil receiver: when `x == nil`, the method returns `mi.MessageOf(x)`, yielding a valid zero-value protobuf reflection rather than panicking. The Rust signature `&self` guarantees a valid reference, meaning any call site that in Go would invoke `ProtoReflect` on a nil `*Hello` (e.g., during crash-recovery deserialization of incomplete state) has no equivalent safe path and would require an `Option` wrapper at the call site that is not present, risking a divergence in crash-recovery scenarios.

title: Lazy message-info initialization elided
message: Go lines 463-466 perform lazy initialization of the message's type metadata via `LoadMessageInfo`/`StoreMessageInfo`, ensuring the global `msgTypes` descriptor is attached to the concrete message state exactly once per instance. The Rust `as_value(self)` call performs no equivalent registration, so if any part of the system later queries cached type metadata on the same instance (as the Go protobuf runtime does for efficient re-serialization and merge operations under the memory-cap-50mb constraint), the Rust port will either redundantly recompute or lack the metadata entirely.

title: Version field flattened from Vector to i64
message: Go's FileDownloadProgressUpdate.Version is *Vector (bep.pb.go:1865), a protobuf message containing Counters []*Counter (bep.pb.go:1239), representing a multi-entry vector clock. The Rust port declares Version as i64 (bep_core.rs:143), a single scalar that cannot represent multiple counter entries. Any DownloadProgress message whose FileDownloadProgressUpdate carries a version vector with more than one counter will lose data during deserialization, breaking wire compatibility and the scenario/index-sequence-behavior and scenario/protocol-state-transition parity tests.

title: Clone returns independent copy vs Go shared slice
message: Go returns []*FileDownloadProgressUpdate (bep.pb.go:1851), a slice of pointers that share identity with the DownloadProgress.Updates backing array; mutations to returned elements are visible through the original struct. The Rust port calls self.Updates.clone() (bep_compat.rs:308), producing a fully independent deep copy. Any caller that mutates an element of the returned Vec expecting the change to propagate back to the DownloadProgress instance will silently diverge from Go behavior, which can surface under scenario/crash-recovery if updates are modified in-place after retrieval.

title: Nil-receiver guard absent
message: Go guards against a nil receiver (bep.pb.go:1852) and returns nil, whereas Rust takes &self (bep_compat.rs:307) which cannot be null. If any call site in Go passes a nil *DownloadProgress and relies on receiving a nil slice (distinct from an empty slice), the Rust port will panic or require an Option wrapper that is not present today. Risk is low because Rust's type system prevents this at compile time, but the nil-vs-empty semantic gap could matter for downstream nil-checks ported from Go.

title: device param type mismatch: DeviceID ([32]byte) vs &str
message: Go's `SetIndexID` accepts `device protocol.DeviceID`, a `[32]byte` fixed-size binary array that enforces a 32-byte length invariant at the type level and preserves arbitrary byte content. The Rust port uses `device: &str`, a variable-length UTF-8 string, which removes the fixed-length guarantee, rejects valid DeviceIDs containing non-UTF-8 byte sequences, and accepts arbitrarily long or short strings that would be impossible in Go. Any caller passing a binary DeviceID that is not valid UTF-8 will fail in Rust but succeed in Go, breaking semantic equivalence on edge-case inputs and wire/schema compatibility when the device parameter is serialized or used as a storage key.

title: Return-type mismatch: void vs bool
message: The Go method `ProtoMessage()` is a no-op marker with no return value (signature `func (*Hello) ProtoMessage() {}`), used solely to satisfy the `proto.Message` interface. The Rust port changes the signature to return `bool` (always `true`). This is a type-fidelity violation: the canonical protobuf marker method is unit-returning, and introducing a `bool` return creates an API surface that does not exist in Go, meaning any Rust caller branching on the return value has no behavioral counterpart and any automated signature-compatibility check (e.g., trait conformance mirroring `proto.Message`) will disagree on the return type.

title: Symbol mismatch in mapping metadata
message: The mapping declares Go symbol `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.Reset` but the Go source window at lines 447-452 contains `Hello.Reset()`, not `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.Reset`; this means either the Rust `Reset` at bep_compat.rs:39 is mapped to the wrong Go method, or the source window is incorrect, and the actual `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.Reset` behavior has not been verified against this Rust implementation.

title: Revert logic replaced with scheduling flags
message: The Go `Revert` method calls `f.doInSync(f.revert)`, which executes the full `revert` function inside a synchronization wrapper. The `revert` method (defined elsewhere in `folder_recvonly.go`) performs the actual revert operation: iterating over locally-changed files, restoring them to the global state, and updating the database. The Rust implementation replaces this entire operation with setting two boolean flags (`scanScheduled` and `pullScheduled`), which merely schedules future scan and pull cycles but never performs the core revert logic—deleting local overrides, restoring global versions, and emitting the corresponding database mutations. This is a complete behavioral gap, not a deferred implementation.

title: Missing doInSync synchronization wrapper
message: The Go code executes the revert through `doInSync`, which acquires the folder's operation lock, runs the provided function, and handles state transitions (e.g., setting folder state to syncing/idle and emitting events). The Rust `Revert` takes `&mut self` but has no equivalent synchronization or state-transition envelope, meaning concurrent callers could observe or mutate inconsistent folder state even if the revert logic were later filled in.

title: Rust function accepts no parameters
message: The Go function signature accepts four parameters (global db.Counts, need db.Counts, sequence int64, state remoteFolderState) and uses them to populate every field of FolderCompletion. The Rust function takes zero parameters, making it impossible to replicate the Go behavior; all callers will receive a default-initialized struct with zero/empty values instead of the computed values derived from global and need counts, the sequence number, and the remote folder state.

title: All field assignments missing
message: The Go function assigns GlobalBytes from global.Bytes, NeedBytes from need.Bytes, GlobalItems as the sum of global.Files+global.Directories+global.Symlinks, NeedItems as the sum of need.Files+need.Directories+need.Symlinks, NeedDeletes from need.Deleted, Sequence from the sequence parameter, and RemoteState from the state parameter. The Rust function returns FolderCompletion::default(), so none of these seven fields are populated, producing semantically wrong completion data for every caller.

title: Missing setCompletionPct call
message: The Go function calls comp.setCompletionPct() at line 834 before returning, which computes the completion percentage from GlobalBytes and NeedBytes (typically as (GlobalBytes-NeedBytes)/GlobalBytes * 100). The Rust function never invokes any equivalent computation, so the CompletionPct field will remain at its default value (likely 0.0) rather than reflecting the actual ratio, breaking all downstream consumers that rely on the completion percentage.

title: Clear-before-persist split-brain on error
message: In the Rust implementation, `self.index_ids.clear()` (line 1092) irrevocably empties the in-memory map before `self.persist_runtime_metadata()` (line 1093) attempts the disk write. If persistence fails, the caller receives an error yet all subsequent in-memory reads via `get_index_id` will return 0, while the on-disk `runtime-meta.json` still contains the old index IDs — a restart would silently restore them. The Go implementation (`internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_indexid.go:87`) uses a single SQL `DELETE FROM indexids` statement which is atomically committed by SQLite; a failure leaves both the query path and the on-disk state unchanged. This split-brain violates the crash-recovery parity requirement and can cause the Rust port to silently skip a full re-index after a failed drop followed by a restart.

title: Output format mismatch: proto text vs JSON
message: Go ClusterConfig.String() at internal/gen/bep/bep.pb.go:581-582 delegates to protoimpl.X.MessageStringOf(x) which produces the protobuf text format representation (e.g. "folders:{id:\"abc\" devices:{...}}"), while the Rust impl_proto_common! macro at bep_compat.rs:43-44 calls as_json_string(self) which delegates to serde_json::to_string producing JSON (e.g. "{\"Folders\":[{\"Id\":\"abc\"}]}"). These are structurally different serialization formats with different field naming conventions (proto uses lowercase snake_case from the .proto schema; serde uses the Rust struct field names which are PascalCase). Any caller that parses, logs, compares, or transmits the String() output will observe incompatible results between Go and Rust, breaking the scenario/protocol-state-transition and scenario/crash-recovery parity tests if String() output is persisted or compared across implementations.

title: Enum String format divergence
message: The Go snippet provided (lines 66-68) is actually MessageType.String() which uses protoimpl.X.EnumStringOf to return the protobuf enum value name (e.g. "MESSAGE_TYPE_CLUSTER_CONFIG" for value 0). The Rust impl_enumish! macro at bep_compat.rs:145-146 uses format!("{}({})", $name, self.Number()) producing "MessageType(0)" instead. This applies to all eight enum types expanded via impl_enumish! (Compression, ErrorCode, FileDownloadProgressUpdateType, FileInfoType, FolderStopReason, FolderType, MessageCompression, MessageType). Any code path that matches on or logs the enum string will see different values, affecting diagnostic output and any enum-name-based protocol negotiation.

title: Nil-receiver divergence
message: Go ClusterConfig.String() has a pointer receiver (*ClusterConfig) and protoimpl.X.MessageStringOf handles nil gracefully by returning an empty string. The Rust implementation at bep_compat.rs:43 takes &self which cannot be null; callers that previously relied on nil-safe String() in Go will need an Option wrapper or will panic if the equivalent None path is exercised. This affects crash-recovery scenarios where partially-initialized state may call String() on a nil message.

title: Output format mismatch: prototext vs JSON
message: Go Index.String() at internal/gen/bep/bep.pb.go:829 calls protoimpl.X.MessageStringOf(x) which produces the canonical protobuf text format (prototext), e.g. folder:"default" files:{name:"foo"} last_sequence:42. The Rust port calls as_json_string(self) which emits serde_json JSON, e.g. {"Folder":"default","Files":[...],"LastSequence":42}. These are entirely different serialization formats with different syntax, different field-name casing (proto snake_case vs Rust PascalCase), and different zero-value elision semantics (proto3 text format omits zero-value fields; serde_json includes them by default). Any code path that logs, compares, or round-trips through String() output will observe incompatible strings, breaking the scenario/protocol-state-transition and scenario/index-sequence-behavior parity tests that depend on consistent stringification.

title: Wrong Go snippet in mapping metadata
message: The mapping header declares the Go symbol as Index.String and cites source lines 66-68, but those lines contain MessageType.String() (an enum helper using EnumStringOf), not Index.String(). The actual Index.String() is at line 829-831 and calls MessageStringOf, a fundamentally different protobuf API. This metadata error could cause reviewers or automated tooling to validate against the wrong Go implementation.

title: Mapping references wrong Go struct
message: The Go source window (lines 852-857) contains `Index.GetFolder`, not the declared target symbol `IndexUpdate.GetFolder`. The actual `IndexUpdate.GetFolder` is at line 914. This mis-mapping means automated parity checks against this mapping ID would validate the wrong Go method, potentially masking regressions in the real `IndexUpdate.GetFolder`.

title: Mapping references wrong Rust impl block
message: The Rust source reference (lines 302-304) points to `DownloadProgress.GetFolder`, not `IndexUpdate.GetFolder`. The actual `IndexUpdate.GetFolder` is at line 730 within `impl IndexUpdate`. Any automated verification trusting this mapping would compare two unrelated structs (`Index` in Go vs `DownloadProgress` in Rust) instead of the intended `IndexUpdate` on both sides.

title: Rust Revert adds side-effects absent in Go no-op
message: The Go method `folder.Revert` at lib/model/folder.go:264 is an intentional no-op (empty body), meaning calling it produces zero state changes. The Rust implementation at syncthing-rs/src/folder_core.rs:496-499 clears `forcedRescanPaths` and sets `scanScheduled = true`, introducing two side effects that have no counterpart in the Go source. This means any caller of `Revert` in the Rust port will mutate folder state (triggering a rescan and discarding forced-rescan paths) where the Go original deliberately does nothing, breaking semantic equivalence and potentially causing spurious rescans or data loss in the `scenario/folder-type-behavior` and `scenario/crash-recovery` parity tests.

title: Return type bool→usize breaks retry semantic
message: Go returns (bool, error) where the bool communicates "true when successful and should not be retried"; Rust returns Result<usize, String> whose Ok variant is usize, not bool. Callers in Go branch on a boolean to decide whether to retry the pull; Rust callers receive a count with no defined mapping to the retry/no-retry decision, so every call-site that inspects the return value will mis-interpret the success signal unless an ad-hoc convention (e.g. 0 vs non-zero) is layered on top, which is undocumented and fragile.

title: Missing context.Context cancellation channel
message: Go's pull accepts a context.Context as its first parameter, enabling cooperative cancellation, deadline propagation, and value passing (e.g. tracing IDs). The Rust signature omits any cancellation token or CancellationToken equivalent, so long-running pulls cannot be cancelled by the caller, breaking behavioral parity in shutdown, folder-pause, and timeout scenarios exercised by the crash-recovery and folder-type-behavior test suites.

title: Extra folder_id parameter widens interface contract
message: Go's puller.pull takes only a context; the puller implementation already knows its folder identity via struct state. The Rust signature adds a folder_id: &str parameter, meaning the caller must supply a folder identifier that does not exist in the Go contract. This shifts responsibility to the caller, risks mismatched folder IDs being passed at call-sites, and makes the trait non-equivalent to the Go interface for any generic dispatch code.

title: Error type loses structured error identity
message: Go returns the error interface, which supports errors.Is/errors.As for typed matching (e.g. distinguishing ErrFolderPaused from network errors). Rust uses plain String, discarding error identity. Downstream code that needs to branch on specific error kinds (as the Go model layer does for conflict-and-ignore-semantics and global-need-decision scenarios) cannot do so without string-matching, which is brittle and locale-sensitive.

title: Return type collapses error and not-found
message: Go returns (protocol.FileInfo, bool, error) at lib/model/model.go:114, providing three distinguishable outcomes: (file, true, nil) for found, (zero, false, nil) for not-found, and (zero, false, err) for errors such as folder-not-configured or DB failure. The Rust implementation returns Option<db::FileInfo>, which collapses the not-found case (bool=false, err=nil) and every error case (err!=nil) into a single None. Callers that must distinguish "folder does not exist" from "file not present in folder" — required by scenario/daemon-api-surface and scenario/folder-type-behavior — cannot do so through this signature.

title: Lock-poisoning error silently swallowed
message: The .ok()? on the RwLock read guard converts a lock-poisoning PoisonError into None, making it indistinguishable from a missing file. The Go equivalent would surface this as an error in the error return value, allowing callers and crash-recovery paths to detect and handle the corruption. This silent swallowing violates scenario/crash-recovery expectations.

title: Visibility narrowed from public to pub(crate)
message: The Go interface method CurrentFolderFile is exported (uppercase) and is part of the public Model interface at lib/model/model.go:114, making it callable by any package including the REST API layer. The Rust function is declared pub(crate), restricting access to the crate only. If the REST/API layer or any external consumer resides in a separate crate, it will not be able to call this method, breaking scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

title: Hardcoded "local" device string instead of typed local device ID
message: The Rust implementation passes the string literal "local" as the device argument to get_device_file. In Go, CurrentFolderFile resolves the local device via protocol.LocalDeviceID, a typed [32]byte constant. If the Rust DB layer does not consistently map the string "local" to the same canonical local-device identity used during index insertion, lookups will silently return None for files that exist, causing silent data loss in scenario/global-need-decision and scenario/index-sequence-behavior.

title: Lazy iterator replaced by eager Vec
message: Go returns `(iter.Seq[protocol.FileInfo], func() error)` — a lazy, streaming iterator with a deferred error callback invoked after iteration completes. Rust returns `Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String>`, which eagerly materializes all results into memory before the caller sees any item and surfaces errors immediately rather than deferring them. This breaks streaming semantics, changes error-reporting timing, and will fail the `scenario/memory-cap-50mb` parity test when the needed-file set is large, since Go can process items one at a time without holding the full result set in memory.

title: Signed limit/offset changed to unsigned usize
message: Go declares `limit, offset int` (signed, 64-bit on 64-bit platforms). Rust uses `usize` (unsigned). In Go, callers can pass negative values (e.g., `limit = -1` is a common idiom meaning "no limit"); the Rust signature cannot represent this, so any call-site that relies on negative sentinel values will either fail to compile or require a different convention, silently altering pagination/filtering behavior.

title: DeviceID downgraded to plain string
message: Go's `device` parameter is `protocol.DeviceID`, a fixed-size 32-byte array with built-in validation, formatting, and binary-safe comparison. Rust accepts `&str`, a variable-length UTF-8 string with no compile-time size or format guarantee. This loses type-level validation, may produce different ordering/comparison semantics for device identifiers, and risks wire/schema incompatibility if the string encoding does not exactly match the canonical Luhn-checksummed base32 representation expected elsewhere.

title: Missing return type and error signaling
message: Go Update returns (bool, error) allowing callers to detect skipped files (false) and propagate database errors; Rust returns unit, so every caller unconditionally treats the file as accepted and no error path exists, breaking all callers that branch on the bool or handle the error.

title: Missing encrypted-parent-directory cleanup
message: Go lines 633-638 check whether the folder is ReceiveEncrypted, the FileInfo is a directory, and the path matches an encrypted parent pattern; if the on-disk directory is empty it is removed and the method returns (false, nil), preventing the item from entering the batch. The Rust implementation has no such guard, so encrypted parent directories will be unconditionally appended to the update batch, corrupting receive-encrypted folder state.

title: Missing GetGlobalFile lookup and receive-only resolution
message: Go lines 641-665 perform a database lookup via GetGlobalFile and execute a four-branch type-switch: propagate DB error, no-op when global not found, call Remove and return (true, nil) when the global item is receive-only-changed and the local item is deleted, and replace the local FileInfo with the global FileInfo when the folder is ReceiveOnly/ReceiveEncrypted and the two are equivalent. The entire block is absent in Rust, meaning deleted receive-only files are never pruned, equivalent local-changed items are never merged back to the global version, and DB errors are silently swallowed.

title: Missing batch Remove call for deleted receive-only items
message: Go line 649 calls b.Remove(fi.Name) to schedule removal of the file when it is deleted and the global entry is a receive-only-changed item. Rust has no Remove invocation, so stale deleted receive-only entries will persist in the database and never be cleaned up, directly affecting the crash-recovery and conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity tests.

title: FileInfo replacement with global entry missing
message: Go line 664 reassigns fi = gf so that the item appended to the batch is the global FileInfo (not the local scan result) when the two are equivalent on a ReceiveOnly or ReceiveEncrypted folder. Rust always pushes the original file argument, so the batch will contain locally-scanned metadata instead of the canonical global metadata, causing version-vector and flag divergence on receive-only folders.

title: Scope changed from all-devices to single-device lookup
message: Go `DeviceStatistics()` takes no arguments and returns statistics for every known device as `map[protocol.DeviceID]stats.DeviceStatistics`. The Rust port adds a `device: &str` parameter and returns data for only that one device. This breaks every caller that expects an aggregate map of all device statistics in a single call and changes the fundamental API contract from "enumerate all" to "point lookup."

title: Structured return type flattened to BTreeMap<String, i64>
message: Go returns `map[protocol.DeviceID]stats.DeviceStatistics` where `stats.DeviceStatistics` is a struct with typed fields (e.g., `LastSeen time.Time`, `LastConnectionDurationS float64`). The Rust port returns `BTreeMap<String, i64>`, collapsing all fields into string-keyed integer values. This loses temporal (`time.Time`) and floating-point (`float64`) fidelity, and any downstream consumer expecting the structured type or its JSON wire representation will see schema-incompatible output.

title: Error return path removed
message: Go's signature returns `(map[…]stats.DeviceStatistics, error)`, allowing callers to distinguish a legitimate empty result from a database or internal failure. The Rust port uses `unwrap_or_default()` and has no `Result` wrapper, silently returning an empty map when the device key is absent or when an underlying error should be surfaced. This violates the error-propagation contract required by parity tests such as `scenario/daemon-api-surface` and `scenario/crash-recovery`.

title: DeviceID key type downgraded to plain String
message: Go uses `protocol.DeviceID` (a fixed-length byte-array type with Luhn check-digit validation and canonical string encoding) as both the outer map key and the `device` parameter type. The Rust port accepts `&str` and keys its return map on `String`, removing all format validation and allowing malformed device IDs to be silently accepted, which can cause wire-level mismatches with the Go daemon.

title: Rust function is enqueue not process
message: The Rust `handleForcedRescans` only inserts paths into `self.forcedRescanPaths`, which is the enqueue/accumulate operation performed by callers *before* the Go method runs. The Go method (lib/model/folder.go:1396-1433) does the opposite: it drains `forcedRescanPaths` into a local slice and resets the map to empty (lines 1397-1403), then processes each path. The Rust implementation is not a port of this method; it is a port of the caller-side path insertion, leaving the actual `handleForcedRescans` logic entirely unimplemented.

title: Missing DB lookup and MustRescan mutation
message: The Go method iterates over drained paths, calls `f.db.GetDeviceFile` to retrieve each file's metadata (line 1417), skips missing files (line 1421-1423), calls `fi.SetMustRescan()` to mark the file for rescan (line 1424), and appends to a `FileInfoBatch` that is flushed to the database via `f.db.Update` (lines 1408-1410, 1428-1430). The Rust implementation performs none of these database operations, meaning files are never actually marked for rescan in the persistent store.

title: Missing scanSubdirs invocation
message: The Go method's final action is `f.scanSubdirs(ctx, paths)` (line 1432), which triggers the actual filesystem rescan of the affected subdirectories. The Rust implementation has no equivalent call, so even if paths are accumulated, no rescan is ever initiated.

title: Missing error propagation and return type
message: The Go method returns `error` and has five distinct error-return sites: `FlushIfFull` (line 1413), `GetDeviceFile` (line 1418), `Flush` (line 1428), and `scanSubdirs` (line 1432). The Rust function returns unit `()`, providing no mechanism for callers to detect or handle failures in batch processing, DB access, or scanning.

title: Missing context cancellation support
message: The Go method accepts `ctx context.Context` (line 1396) which is threaded to `scanSubdirs` for cooperative cancellation. The Rust signature has no cancellation token, `CancellationToken`, or async context equivalent, so long-running rescans cannot be cancelled.

title: Missing batch flush-if-full logic
message: The Go method uses a `FileInfoBatch` with `FlushIfFull` (line 1413) to cap memory usage during large rescan sets, flushing to the DB in bounded increments. The Rust implementation has no batching mechanism, which will become a correctness and memory issue once the DB-update logic is added, particularly relevant to the required `memory-cap-50mb` parity test.

title: DeviceID typed parameter replaced with bare &str
message: Go `ConnectedTo` accepts `protocol.DeviceID`, a strongly-typed `[32]byte` array with defined encoding (base32 + Luhn check groups). The Rust port accepts `&str`, losing all type-level guarantees: callers can pass arbitrary strings, no validation occurs, and correctness depends entirely on the connections map using the exact same string serialization that Go's `DeviceID.String()` produces. If any code path inserts keys using a different format (e.g., raw hex, lowercase, no dashes), the lookup silently returns `false` instead of matching—a behavioral divergence that would be a compile-time error in the Go version.

title: Visibility downgraded from public interface to pub(crate)
message: Go `ConnectedTo` is declared on the exported `Model` interface (lib/model/model.go:123), making it part of the public API surface consumed by REST handlers, the GUI backend, and other packages. The Rust method is marked `pub(crate)`, restricting it to crate-internal use only. Any external consumer that relied on this method through the `Model` trait (e.g., the daemon API surface tested by `scenario/daemon-api-surface`) would fail to compile, breaking API parity.

title: Stub implementation missing all categorization logic
message: The Rust function is a trivial loop that calls handleFile on each path and counts successes. The Go method (lines 326-427) sorts needed files into multiple categories — ignored files, Windows-invalid filenames, deleted directories, deleted symlinks, deleted regular files, regular files needing pull, unsupported symlinks on Windows/Android, directories, and symlinks — and dispatches each to distinct handlers (deleteFile, deleteFileWithCurrent, shortcutFile, handleDir, handleSymlink, queue.Push, dbUpdateChan sends). None of this dispatch logic exists in Rust.

title: Missing file queue processing with rename optimization
message: Go lines 437-513 pop files from a priority queue and attempt a rename-from-deletion-candidate optimization using BlocksHash buckets before falling back to handleFile. It also checks file availability across devices (fileAvailability) and disk space (CheckAvailableSpace) before processing. The Rust implementation has no queue, no rename optimization, no availability check, and no space check.

title: Missing ignore and IgnoreDelete semantics
message: Go lines 337-346 skip deleted files when IgnoreDelete is configured and mark pattern-ignored files as invalid via dbUpdateChan. The Rust function performs no ignore matching and no IgnoreDelete check, breaking the conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity test.

title: Return type mismatch loses deletion state
message: Go returns (map[string]protocol.FileInfo, []protocol.FileInfo, error) — a map of pending file deletions and a slice of directory deletions that callers use to perform deletions after file pulls complete. Rust returns usize (a count), discarding all deletion state required by downstream processing.

title: Missing context cancellation checks
message: Go checks ctx.Done() at three points: inside the main loop (line 331), after the loop (line 429), and inside the queue loop (line 439), returning ctx.Err() on cancellation. The Rust function has no cancellation mechanism, which means it cannot be interrupted and will not propagate cancellation errors, affecting crash-recovery semantics.

title: Missing error propagation from database lookups
message: Go calls sdb.GetDeviceFile (lines 370, 389) and sdb.GetGlobalFile (line 450) with explicit error returns that abort the entire function on failure. The Rust function signature has no error return path (returns usize), silently swallowing any errors from handleFile via is_ok(), preventing proper crash-recovery behavior.

title: Missing Windows/Android platform-specific branches
message: Go lines 348-359 and 403-410 handle Windows-invalid filenames and unsupported symlinks on Windows/Android by either recording pull errors or marking files as unsupported. These platform branches are entirely absent in Rust, which would cause behavioral divergence on those platforms.

title: Return type discards errors
message: Go `scanSubdirs` returns `error` to the caller, allowing upstream logic (scheduling, retry) to react to scan failures. Rust returns `()` and silently swallows errors via `newScanError`, making it impossible for callers to distinguish success from failure. This breaks any caller that gates behavior on the scan result.

title: Missing ignore-hash-change deferred pull
message: Go lines 453-470 capture the ignore-pattern hash before scanning and, on exit, compare it to the new hash; if changed, it calls `ignoresUpdated()` and `SchedulePull()`. Rust has no equivalent, so ignore-pattern changes during a scan will not trigger a re-pull, causing newly-unignored files to remain un-synced until the next manual trigger.

title: Missing folder state transitions
message: Go sets state to `FolderScanWaiting` (line 472), then `FolderScanning` (line 510), and defers `FolderIdle` (line 473). Rust performs no state transitions at all, so any component or UI that observes folder state will never see scanning activity and may make incorrect scheduling decisions.

title: Missing batch flush between phases
message: Go creates a `scanBatch` (line 513) and explicitly flushes it after Phase 1 (line 540) and after Phase 2 (line 565), ensuring database writes from changed-and-new detection are persisted before deleted-and-ignored detection runs. Rust has no visible batch or flush, risking Phase 2 operating on stale data or accumulated writes being lost on early return.

title: Missing empty-subDirs normalization between phases
message: Go lines 545-549 check after Phase 1 whether `subDirs` is empty and, if so, reset it to `[""]` so Phase 2 traverses the entire folder tree. Rust passes the same `normalized` slice to both phases unchanged, so a full-folder scan may cause Phase 2 to skip traversal entirely if `normalized` is empty.

title: Phase 2 missing cfiler and alreadyUsedOrExisting
message: Go Phase 1 (line 531) returns a `cfiler` and `alreadyUsedOrExisting` set that are passed into Phase 2 (line 556) to coordinate which files have already been processed and to provide current-file information. Rust Phase 2 receives neither, so it cannot distinguish already-processed files from truly deleted ones, risking false deletion detection.

title: Missing ScanCompleted call
message: Go calls `f.ScanCompleted()` (line 570) on the success path, which typically updates sequence numbers and notifies watchers. Rust never calls any equivalent, so downstream components waiting on scan completion will not be notified.

title: Missing IO limiter acquisition
message: Go acquires an IO limiter permit with context cancellation (lines 475-478), bounding concurrent disk IO across folders. Rust has no rate-limiting, allowing unbounded concurrent scans that may saturate disk IO.

title: Missing setError(nil) on healthy start
message: Go line 459 calls `f.setError(ctx, nil)` after a successful health check to clear any previously recorded folder error. Rust does not clear prior errors, so a folder that recovers from a transient problem will continue to appear errored.

title: Missing clearScanErrors before scan
message: Go line 511 calls `f.clearScanErrors(subDirs)` to reset per-file scan errors for the directories about to be scanned. Rust has no equivalent, so stale scan errors from previous runs will persist and may mislead users or other components.

title: Error return dropped silently
message: Go signature returns error and short-circuits before emitDiskChangeEvents when updateLocals fails (lib/model/folder.go:1321-1322), but the Rust function returns unit and on DB-persist failure (line 1181-1184) merely logs via newScanError then falls through to emitDiskChangeEvents (line 1190) and forcedRescanPaths removal (lines 1187-1189), so callers cannot detect the failure and disk-change events fire for data that was never persisted to the database.

title: Input type contract changed
message: Go accepts []protocol.FileInfo (pre-built structured file metadata) and delegates directly to updateLocals, whereas Rust accepts &[String] (raw paths) and internally constructs file snapshots via build_local_file_snapshot with manual sequence numbering (lines 1150-1171), shifting metadata-construction responsibility into this function and changing the caller contract.

title: Event type argument missing
message: Go passes two arguments to emitDiskChangeEvents: the file-info slice and events.LocalChangeDetected (lib/model/folder.go:1324), but the Rust call passes only the scanned slice with no event-type discriminator, risking the wrong event type being emitted or the LocalChangeDetected vs RemoteChangeDetected distinction being lost.

title: Events emitted for unfiltered input
message: Rust calls emitDiskChangeEvents with the original unfiltered scanned slice, which includes paths that were skipped by should_skip_scan_path (line 1160) and paths whose build_local_file_snapshot failed (line 1170), whereas Go passes only the FileInfo objects that were successfully provided to updateLocals, so the Rust version can emit spurious change events for items never persisted.

title: Extra forcedRescanPaths removal
message: The Rust version unconditionally removes every scanned path from forcedRescanPaths, but the Go snippet contains no such logic in updateLocalsFromScanning; if this behavior was relocated from another call site it should be verified, otherwise it adds side effects absent from the Go function.

title: limit type signed-vs-unsigned
message: Go declares `limit int` (signed, 64-bit on 64-bit platforms) which allows negative sentinel values (e.g., -1 commonly meaning "no limit") while Rust declares `limit: usize` (unsigned). Any Go caller passing a negative limit to signal unbounded iteration has no equivalent representation in the Rust port, silently changing semantics or causing a panic at the call site.

title: eager Vec vs lazy iterator
message: Go returns `(iter.Seq[protocol.FileInfo], func() error)` — a lazy, streaming iterator that yields items on demand and defers error reporting until the caller invokes the error closure after partial or full consumption. Rust returns `Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String>`, which eagerly materializes the entire result set into memory before returning. This breaks streaming semantics, changes memory profile (directly relevant to the memory-cap-50mb parity test), and eliminates the ability to partially consume results before encountering an error.

title: deferred vs immediate error model
message: Go's `func() error` return allows the caller to iterate over available results and then check for an error that occurred during iteration (partial success is observable). Rust's `Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String>` is all-or-nothing: callers receive either the complete set or an error, with no partial-result path. This is a missing behavior branch that affects crash-recovery and index-sequence-behavior scenarios where partial iteration followed by error inspection is meaningful.

title: device parameter type fidelity
message: Go uses `protocol.DeviceID` (a structured type, typically a 32-byte array with specific binary encoding and validation) for the `device` parameter. Rust uses `&str`, a plain UTF-8 string with no structural validation. This loses type-level guarantees around device identity encoding and could cause wire/schema incompatibility if DeviceID's binary representation does not round-trip cleanly through a string encoding.

title: Override adds spurious pullScheduled side-effect
message: Go's `folder.Override()` (lib/model/folder.go:262) is an intentional no-op on the base `folder` type, serving as a default that concrete folder types (e.g. `sendReceiveFolder`) may override with real logic. The Rust port at folder_core.rs:493 unconditionally sets `self.pullScheduled = true`, introducing a pull-scheduling side-effect that does not exist in the Go base implementation. Any caller invoking `Override()` on a folder that should be a no-op will now erroneously schedule a pull, which can affect folder-type-behavior and global-need-decision parity tests.

title: scanErrors field conflated with errorsMut mutex
message: In Go, `errorsMut` is a `sync.Mutex` protecting the `scanErrors` slice, and errors are appended to `f.scanErrors` (lib/model/folder.go:1268). In Rust, errors are pushed directly onto `self.errorsMut` (line 667), treating the mutex name as the error list. This means the Rust struct has no separate `scanErrors` collection; any code reading `scanErrors` elsewhere will not find accumulated errors, and the data model diverges from Go's two-field (mutex + slice) design.

title: Missing warning log side effect
message: The Go method emits a structured warning log `f.sl.Warn("Failed to scan", slogutil.FilePath(path), slogutil.Error(err))` on every call (lib/model/folder.go:1267). The Rust implementation performs no logging at all. This removes an observable side effect that operators rely on for diagnosing scan failures and that parity tests under scenario/crash-recovery and scenario/folder-type-behavior may assert on.

title: No concurrency guard around error list mutation
message: The Go method explicitly acquires `f.errorsMut.Lock()` before mutating and releases it after (lib/model/folder.go:1266,1272), protecting concurrent access from multiple goroutines. The Rust method takes `&mut self`, which provides compile-time single-owner exclusivity but does not enforce runtime mutual exclusion if the struct is wrapped in `Arc<RwLock<…>>` or similar shared-ownership container. If callers share this struct across async tasks or threads (as the Go code implies), the locking semantics must be enforced at the call site or via an interior-mutex wrapper; neither is visible here.

title: Error parameter type narrows from error interface to bare string
message: The Go parameter `err error` is an interface; the method calls `err.Error()` to extract the string (lib/model/folder.go:1269). The Rust parameter is already `&str`, so the caller must pre-convert. This shifts responsibility and loses the ability to inspect the original error type inside `newScanError`, which could matter if future logic branches on error kind or wraps the error.

title: Clone-vs-reference return semantics
message: Go's GetFiles (bep.pb.go:861) returns the internal slice by reference, meaning callers share the same backing array and any mutation to elements or slice length is visible to the Index owner. The Rust port performs a full deep clone via `self.Files.clone()`, so callers receive an independent copy. Any Go call-site that appends to, truncates, or mutates elements of the returned slice to implicitly update the parent Index message will silently have no effect in the Rust port, breaking behavioral equivalence for scenarios such as crash-recovery and index-sequence-behavior that may rely on in-place mutation of file lists.

title: Pointer indirection flattened to owned values
message: Go returns `[]*FileInfo` (bep.pb.go:859) — a slice of pointers — so two Index messages can share the same FileInfo object by pointer, and identity/aliasing comparisons (`==` on pointer) are meaningful. The Rust signature returns `Vec<FileInfo>` (owned values), collapsing pointer identity into value semantics. Any protocol-state-transition or index-sequence-behavior scenario that relies on shared FileInfo references across multiple Index or IndexUpdate messages will diverge because the Rust side always produces distinct copies.

title: Nil receiver guard absent
message: Go's generated getter guards against a nil receiver (`if x != nil`, bep.pb.go:860) and returns nil, which is idiomatic in protobuf Go and exercised when an uninitialized or zeroed message pointer is accessed. The Rust method takes `&self` with no Option wrapper, so there is no equivalent code path that returns an empty/default value for a missing Index. If any translated call-site previously relied on nil-safe access (e.g., calling GetFiles on an Option-wrapped Index without unwrapping), it will panic at runtime rather than returning an empty Vec, which could surface under crash-recovery scenarios.

title: Lazy iterator replaced by eager Vec
message: Go returns (iter.Seq[protocol.FileInfo], func() error) at interface.go:40, a lazy pull-based iterator with a deferred error callback that is invoked after iteration completes. Rust returns Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String> at db.rs:154, which forces eager collection of every matching file into memory before the caller sees any result. This breaks streaming semantics: Go callers can abort mid-iteration without materializing the full result set, whereas Rust always materializes it. This is a concrete behavioral divergence that will violate the scenario/memory-cap-50mb parity test for large folders and changes error-observation timing from deferred to upfront.

title: DeviceID typed parameter downgraded to plain string
message: Go's device parameter is protocol.DeviceID, a [32]byte newtype with Luhn-based check-digit validation and a specific base32 wire encoding (see protocol.DeviceID). Rust accepts &str at db.rs:154, losing compile-time type safety and check-digit validation at the interface boundary. Any caller passing an un-validated or differently-encoded device string will silently produce wrong lookups or empty results instead of a type error, breaking wire/schema compatibility (Check 5).

title: Deferred error callback collapsed into Result
message: Go's second return value func() error (interface.go:40) is called after the iterator is drained, reporting errors that occurred during iteration (e.g., corrupt key decoding mid-scan). Rust's Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String> conflates iteration errors with initialization errors and cannot represent partial success — if an error occurs after N items are decoded, Go surfaces those N items plus the error, while Rust surfaces only the error. This is a missing behavior path (Check 4) that affects crash-recovery and index-sequence-behavior scenarios.

title: Nil-receiver guard absent
message: The Go method (bep.pb.go:853-856) returns the zero value (empty string) when the receiver pointer is nil, providing safe default behavior for unset optional protobuf message fields. The Rust implementation takes `&self`, which structurally prevents nil calls but does not encode the nil-to-default-value fallback. If callers hold an `Option<Index>` (the Rust equivalent of a nil `*Index`), they must manually supply the empty-string default; this unguarded responsibility shift risks panics or incorrect behavior if callers unwrap without defaulting, and no documentation or wrapper enforces the contract.

title: Visibility restricted to pub(crate)
message: The Go getter `GetFolder` is an exported (uppercase, public) method available to any external package, consistent with protobuf-generated Go code conventions. The Rust port declares it `pub(crate)`, restricting access to the defining crate only. Any external crate consuming the `bep-compat` component that needs to call `GetFolder` on an `Index` value—as would be expected for protobuf message accessors—will fail to compile.

title: Missing local-device IndexID auto-generation
message: The Go implementation at internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_indexid.go:31-56 checks whether the caller is the local device (protocol.LocalDeviceID) and, when no IndexID exists, auto-generates a new random IndexID via protocol.NewIndexID() (rand.Uint64()), persists it with an INSERT that seeds the sequence column to COALESCE(MAX(sequence),0), and returns the newly created ID. The Rust implementation unconditionally returns 0 via unwrap_or(&0) for any missing entry, never generating or persisting a new ID. This breaks the Syncthing index exchange protocol, which requires the local device to always have a valid non-zero IndexID; callers will receive 0 and treat the folder as never-indexed.

title: Device parameter type loses validation and canonical form
message: Go's GetIndexID accepts protocol.DeviceID, a [32]byte value type with a canonical String() representation (base32 + Luhn check digits + hyphen chunking) used for SQL lookups at internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_indexid.go:27. The Rust signature accepts &str with no validation or normalization, so a non-canonical string (e.g., lowercase, missing check digits, different chunking) would silently produce a different BTreeMap key, causing lookups to miss entries that the Go implementation would find, and preventing the identity comparison with LocalDeviceID that gates auto-generation.

title: Entire method body is unimplemented stub
message: The Rust function performs only a trivial filter of `forcedRescanPaths` by subdirectory coverage and returns a removal count. The Go function (lib/model/folder.go:786-921) drains deleted files from a streaming CFiler, checks each against ignore patterns, processes a multi-branch switch handling four distinct cases (already-ignored, newly-ignored with directory-delayed batching, newly-deleted with conflict version clearing, and deleted-receive-only-changed with global file lookup), issues batch updates/removes to the database, flushes the batch when full, and counts actual DB changes. None of this logic exists in the Rust port—no ignore matching, no SetDeleted/SetIgnored, no batch Update/Remove/FlushIfFull, no conflict version zeroing, no receive-only flag clearing, and no global file lookup.

title: Missing critical parameters and dependencies
message: The Go signature accepts `ctx context.Context`, `batch *scanBatch`, `cfiler *streamingCFiler`, and `alreadyUsedOrExisting map[string]struct{}` (lib/model/folder.go:776). The Rust function takes only `subdirs: &[String]`, so it has no access to a cancellation context, no scan batch for DB writes, no streaming CFiler to drain deleted files from, and no set of already-processed names to skip. Without these, the core scan-phase-2 workflow cannot be performed.

title: Empty-subdirs early return inverts Go behavior
message: When `subdirs` is empty the Rust function clears `forcedRescanPaths` and returns `Ok(0)`, skipping all processing. In Go (lib/model/folder.go:792), the subdir filter is only applied when `len(subDirs) > 0 && subDirs[0] != ""`; when subdirs is empty or the first element is the empty string, all deleted files are processed without any prefix filter. The Rust behavior therefore suppresses the entire scan for the "full scan" case.

title: Subdirectory prefix matching semantics differ
message: The Rust code requires an exact match (`pending == sub`) or a `/`-delimited prefix (`pending.starts_with(&format!("{sub}/"))`). The Go code at lib/model/folder.go:795 uses `strings.HasPrefix(fi.Name, sub)` with no separator requirement, so a subdirectory `"foo"` in Go matches file `"foobar"` while in Rust it does not. This changes which files are included in the scan scope.

title: Return value semantic mismatch
message: The Rust function returns a count of entries removed from `forcedRescanPaths`, while the Go function (lib/model/folder.go:948) returns the number of actual database changes (batch updates and removes) made during deleted/ignored file processing. Callers relying on the change count to decide whether to commit or announce changes will receive a meaningless value from the Rust port.

title: Global-winner algorithm uses sequence not version-vectors
message: Go pre-computes the global winner during updates in recalcGlobalForFile (folderdb_update.go:388-397) using fileRow.Compare (folderdb_update.go:473-511) which ranks candidates by version-vector comparison (Equal/Greater/Lesser/ConcurrentGreater/ConcurrentLesser), then invalid-flag status, then device-index for ties, and modified-time for concurrent conflicts. Rust prefer_global (db.rs:1351-1362) instead ranks candidates by highest sequence number, then non-deleted over deleted, then most-recent modified_ns, then lexicographic path. Because version vectors are not consulted, two devices with concurrent but different version vectors will select a different global winner in Rust vs Go, breaking semantic equivalence of get_global_file for any multi-device folder with conflicts.

title: Missing Unicode NFC normalization of file path
message: Go GetGlobalFile calls osutil.NormalizedFilename(file) at folderdb_global.go:23 which applies NFC normalization (and on Windows also slash conversion) before looking up the file. Rust get_global_file at db.rs:688-690 passes the file argument directly to global_winner_for_path with no normalization. Any non-NFC-encoded filename (common on macOS which stores NFD) will fail to match stored entries in Rust while succeeding in Go.

title: All-ignored entries return None instead of fallback winner
message: Go recalcGlobalForFile at folderdb_update.go:393-397 explicitly handles the case where all file entries across devices are invalid: it falls back to selecting the first entry after sorting (globIdx = 0), always producing a valid global FileInfo. Rust global_winner_for_path at db.rs:489-491 unconditionally skips entries where meta.ignored is true and returns Ok(None) at line 504 if no non-ignored candidate remains. When every device entry for a file is ignored, Go returns (FileInfo, true, nil) while Rust returns Ok(None), a semantic divergence observable in the global-need-decision and index-sequence-behavior parity tests.

title: Missing updateBatch flush callback
message: The Go implementation at folder.go:587-594 initializes `updateBatch` with a `NewFileInfoBatch` callback that (a) checks folder health via `getHealthErrorWithoutIgnores()`, returning an error and logging if unhealthy, and (b) calls `updateLocalsFromScanning(fs)` to persist scanned file metadata. The Rust implementation uses `..Default::default()` and never sets up any equivalent callback or flush function. This means scanned file updates will never be applied to local state, and mid-scan health checks are entirely absent, breaking core scan semantics.

title: Folder reference downgraded to bare string ID
message: Go stores a full folder reference (`f: f` at folder.go:584) in the `scanBatch`, giving the batch's callback access to methods like `getHealthErrorWithoutIgnores()` and `updateLocalsFromScanning()`. Rust stores only `self.config.id.clone()`, a plain string. Even if the missing callback were later added, the batch would lack the object reference needed to invoke health checks or persist scan results, requiring an architectural change to restore parity.

title: toRemove not pre-allocated to maxToRemove capacity
message: Go explicitly pre-allocates `toRemove` with `make([]string, 0, maxToRemove)` at folder.go:585, reserving capacity to avoid repeated allocations during scanning. The Rust side relies on `Default::default()`, which typically yields a zero-capacity `Vec`. While not a correctness bug, this diverges from the Go behavior under memory-cap-50mb test scenarios where allocation patterns matter, and could cause excess reallocations during large scans.

title: Revert body replaces doInSync(revert) with flag-setting
message: The Go method delegates to doInSync(f.revert), which acquires synchronization, executes the real revert logic (the f.revert method containing file-set reversion, index update, and completion tracking), and then releases synchronization. The Rust implementation instead only sets two boolean scheduling flags (scanScheduled and pullScheduled) without invoking any revert logic or synchronization wrapper, meaning the actual folder reversion (reverting the file set to the global state, sending index updates, etc.) never occurs. This is a complete behavioral mismatch, not a stub or partial implementation, because the flags it sets are unrelated to the revert operation's contract.

title: Missing doInSync synchronization wrapper
message: The Go Revert method wraps its work inside doInSync, which serializes folder operations (typically acquiring a mutex, checking folder state, deferring state restoration, and running the callback). The Rust version takes only &mut self with no equivalent serialization guard, so concurrent callers could observe or mutate inconsistent folder state if this method is ever invoked outside an already-held exclusive lock, violating the synchronization contract of the Go original.

title: Signature arity mismatch: Rust adds device parameter
message: Go `ConnectionStats()` accepts no arguments and returns aggregate stats for all connections as `map[string]interface{}`. The Rust port adds a `device: &str` parameter and performs a single-key lookup via `self.connections.get(device)`, changing the method from a bulk-query over all connections to a single-device lookup. Any caller expecting the full map of all connection statistics will receive only one entry (or None), breaking API parity and every consumer that iterates the returned map.

title: Return type mismatch: single Option vs full map
message: Go returns `map[string]interface{}`, a heterogeneous map of every connected device's stats keyed by device identifier string. Rust returns `Option<ConnectionStats>`, a typed single-device result. This loses the ability to enumerate all connections in one call, changes the error representation (missing device yields `None` instead of an absent map key), and replaces the loosely-typed `interface{}` values with a concrete `ConnectionStats` struct, which may omit dynamic keys that the Go side inserts into the map at runtime.

title: Visibility restricts public interface method to crate-internal
message: Go declares `ConnectionStats` on the exported `Model` interface, making it part of the public API surface. The Rust port marks it `pub(crate)`, preventing external crates (including the daemon API layer and any test harness outside the crate) from calling it, which will break the `scenario/daemon-api-surface` parity test.

title: device param type mismatch
message: Go `GetDeviceSequence` accepts `device protocol.DeviceID` (a strongly-typed 32-byte identifier with defined marshalling, comparison, and validation semantics), but the Rust `get_device_sequence` accepts `device: &str`, a plain string with no compile-time type safety. Other methods in the same Rust trait (e.g. `list_devices_for_folder` at line 199, `remote_sequences` at line 200) correctly use the `DeviceId` type for device identifiers. This inconsistency means callers can pass arbitrary strings without validation, and if the underlying implementation constructs database keys from the device value, a string-encoded device ID may produce different key bytes than the Go binary `DeviceID` representation, causing lookup misses or data isolation failures across the two implementations.

title: Wrong data source for sequences
message: Go queries the `indexids` table (`SELECT d.device_id, i.sequence FROM indexids i INNER JOIN devices d ...` at folderdb_indexid.go:117-120) to return per-device index-acknowledged sequence numbers. Rust instead scans every file record in the folder via `store.for_each_file_in_folder` and computes `max(file.sequence)` per device. These are semantically different values: `indexids.sequence` is an explicitly stored index-level sequence that may lag behind or differ from the maximum file-level sequence, especially after partial updates, crash recovery, or when index IDs are set independently of file ingestion. This will cause divergent results in scenario/crash-recovery and scenario/index-sequence-behavior tests.

title: Local device not excluded
message: Go's SQL includes the filter `WHERE i.device_idx != {{.LocalDeviceIdx}}` (folderdb_indexid.go:119), ensuring the local device is excluded from the returned map. The Rust implementation iterates all devices in the folder store (store.rs:362 `for (device, path_store) in device_store`) without any filter, so the local device's max sequence is included in the returned BTreeMap. Callers such as folder_summary that display remote-only device sequences will show an extra spurious entry for the local device.

title: Return type breaks reflection contract
message: Go returns `protoreflect.Message`, a full reflection handle exposing field descriptors, presence checks, dynamic get/set, and the parent `MessageDescriptor`. Rust returns `Value` (a serialized data snapshot) which provides none of these capabilities. Any consumer that calls `ProtoReflect()` to walk field descriptors, check field presence, or round-trip through the protobuf reflection API will get a type mismatch or missing functionality. This breaks wire/schema compatibility for any code path that relies on descriptor-driven serialization or dynamic field access.

title: Nil-receiver branch absent
message: Go lines 462-469 handle a nil receiver: when `x == nil`, the method returns `mi.MessageOf(x)`, yielding a valid type-level reflection message that carries the `MessageDescriptor` without instance data. This is used by protobuf internals and user code to obtain type metadata from a nil typed pointer (e.g., `(*ClusterConfig)(nil).ProtoReflect().Descriptor()`). The Rust signature `&self` makes this path unreachable; there is no equivalent way to obtain the message descriptor without a live instance, so any Go call site that invokes `ProtoReflect` on a nil pointer to extract type-level metadata has no Rust counterpart.

title: Eager Vec replaces lazy iterator, breaks streaming semantics
message: Go returns `(iter.Seq[protocol.FileInfo], func() error)` — a lazy, pull-based iterator that yields one FileInfo at a time without materializing the full result set, plus a deferred error callback checked after iteration. Rust returns `Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String>`, eagerly collecting every matching file into memory before returning. This eliminates streaming capability, changes memory profile from O(1) to O(n) in the number of matching files, and will violate the `scenario/memory-cap-50mb` parity test for large prefix matches. It also collapses the two-phase error model: in Go a caller can receive partial results and then discover an I/O error via the close function, whereas in Rust partial results are impossible — the call either fully succeeds or fully fails.

title: Device parameter loses DeviceID type safety
message: Go declares the device parameter as `protocol.DeviceID`, a strongly-typed 32-byte identifier with its own marshaling, validation, and `String()` representation. Rust accepts a plain `&str`, deferring all validation to the implementation and permitting malformed device identifiers to reach the storage layer. Other methods in the same Rust trait (e.g. `list_devices_for_folder` at line 199) already use `DeviceId` as a return type, so the input parameter should also use `DeviceId` (or `&DeviceId`) for consistency and to prevent encoding mismatches on the key path.

title: Error type downgraded from interface to String
message: Go's `func() error` returns a value satisfying the `error` interface, which in practice carries structured context (e.g., `*os.PathError`, wrapped sentinel errors queryable with `errors.Is`/`errors.As`). Rust uses `String`, which discards error classification and makes it impossible for callers to programmatically distinguish I/O errors from not-found or corruption errors — a behavioral difference relevant to the `scenario/crash-recovery` and `scenario/wal-free-durability` tests that may need to branch on error kind.

title: Return type mismatch: EnumDescriptor vs &str
message: The Go method returns a protoreflect.EnumDescriptor (bep.pb.go:70), a rich interface that exposes the full protobuf enum schema including value enumeration, options, full qualified name, parent file descriptor, and source-location metadata. The Rust port returns &'static str (bep_compat.rs:27), a plain string that cannot satisfy any reflection query against the descriptor. Any downstream code that calls Descriptor() to introspect enum values, check deprecation flags, resolve source info, or walk the descriptor tree will silently receive a degraded result, breaking wire-schema introspection parity and potentially failing the protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior scenarios that depend on descriptor-driven dispatch.

title: Receiver semantics divergence: enum-level vs instance-level
message: The Go Descriptor is defined on the enum type itself (func (MessageType) Descriptor(), bep.pb.go:70) with a value receiver, meaning it does not use the receiver value and always returns the single canonical descriptor for the enum type. The Rust version takes &self (bep_compat.rs:27) and passes $name into a helper, making the return value potentially vary per-instance depending on how the macro is instantiated. If the descriptor() helper is keyed on a per-variant name rather than the enum-level type name, different enum variants could return different descriptor strings, which is incorrect — Go always returns the same file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes[0] descriptor regardless of variant.

title: delegate target opacity: file-registered descriptor vs freestanding helper
message: Go's implementation delegates to file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes[0].Descriptor() (bep.pb.go:71), which is a file-level registry entry populated at init time from the raw proto file descriptor bytes, guaranteeing the returned descriptor is identical to the schema embedded in the binary. The Rust version calls descriptor($name) (bep_compat.rs:28) whose provenance is unverified in this snippet; if descriptor() constructs or looks up metadata by string name rather than from the compiled proto file descriptor, the result can drift from the canonical schema, causing crash-recovery and memory-cap-50mb scenarios to observe inconsistent metadata.

title: device param type mismatch
message: Go `DropDevice` accepts `protocol.DeviceID`, a structured validated type (typically a fixed-size byte array with Luhn-check encoding), but Rust `drop_device` accepts `&str`. The Rust codebase already defines a `DeviceId` type (used in return positions at lines 199 and 200), yet the input parameter here is a bare string. This breaks type-level validation: callers can pass malformed or empty strings that Go would reject at compile time, and key-encoding in the storage layer may silently produce wrong keys if the string is not in the canonical DeviceID format.

title: Missing nil-receiver guard
message: The Go method (bep.pb.go:612) contains an explicit nil-receiver check that returns the zero value `false` when `x` is nil, which is idiomatic for protobuf-generated Go getters and is relied upon by callers that may hold a nil `*ClusterConfig`. The Rust implementation takes `&self`, which cannot be called on a None value, but provides no companion free-function or `Option`-aware wrapper that replicates the nil-returns-false semantics. If any call site in the ported code holds an `Option<ClusterConfig>` and unconditionally invokes `GetSecondary` (mirroring Go's nil-safe call pattern), it will panic or fail to compile rather than silently returning `false`, diverging from the Go behavior.

title: Struct receiver mismatch
message: The mapping declares the Go symbol as `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.GetName` but the Go source at line 749 defines the method on `*Device`, not `*FileDownloadProgressUpdate`. If the Rust implementation at syncthing-rs/src/bep_compat.rs:289 is attached to a `FileDownloadProgressUpdate` struct rather than a `Device` struct, the getter is bound to the wrong protobuf message type, which will cause field misrouting and wire-level incompatibility when serializing or accessing the `Name` field across these distinct message types.

title: Missing initialScanFinished gate
message: Go blocks on the initialScanFinished channel at line 303 before proceeding, ensuring no user-triggered scan runs until the initial scan completes. The Rust Scan method has no equivalent await or blocking mechanism, allowing scans to execute before initialization finishes, which can cause data races against the initial scan or operate on incomplete state.

title: Missing doInSync serialization
message: Go wraps the entire scan body in doInSync (line 304), which serializes folder operations so that only one sync-related action runs at a time. The Rust implementation has no equivalent mutual-exclusion wrapper, meaning concurrent Scan calls or interleaved folder operations can race on shared mutable state such as pullScheduled and scanScheduled.

title: Error return type dropped
message: Go Scan returns error (line 302), propagating failures from scanSubdirs back to the caller so it can log, retry, or notify. The Rust signature returns unit; the Err branch at line 517 stores the error internally via newScanError but never surfaces it to the caller, silently swallowing failures that Go callers are expected to handle.

title: Divergent method body with inlined extra logic
message: Go Scan delegates entirely to scanSubdirs (line 305) and does nothing else. The Rust Scan calls scanSubdirs at line 508 and then performs additional operations: clearScanErrors (line 507, executed before scanSubdirs), normalize_scan_subdirs (line 509), scan_filesystem_deterministic (line 511), conditional pullScheduled assignment (line 514), and scanScheduled reset (line 519). This either duplicates work already inside scanSubdirs or introduces behavior not present in the Go Scan path, changing observable side effects and execution order.
