title: Missing context-based cancellation
message: Go passes a context.Context obtained from doInSync into scanSubdirs (line 305), enabling cooperative cancellation when the folder is stopped or the service shuts down. The Rust scanSubdirs call at line 508 accepts no cancellation token or equivalent, so long-running scans cannot be interrupted, risking hangs during shutdown.

title: Missing updateLocals call
message: The Go method calls f.updateLocals(fs) at line 1329 to persist the pulled file metadata into the local database and propagates any error from that call. The Rust implementation entirely omits this call, replacing it with a loop that removes entries from forcedRescanPaths (lines 1143-1145), which is unrelated behavior not present in the Go source. This means pulled files are never recorded in the local index, breaking synchronization state.

title: Wrong input type loses file metadata
message: The Go method accepts []protocol.FileInfo (rich structs carrying name, size, version vector, block hashes, permissions, etc.) while the Rust method accepts &[String] (bare path strings). This type mismatch means the Rust side cannot forward full file metadata to updateLocals or emitDiskChangeEvents, making faithful reimplementation of either downstream call impossible without a signature change.

title: Error propagation removed
message: The Go method returns error and propagates failures from updateLocals (lines 1329-1331). The Rust method returns unit and silently discards the Result from emitDiskChangeEvents via let _ = (line 1146). Callers in Go can detect and react to persistence failures; Rust callers cannot, which may cause silent data-loss scenarios tested by scenario/crash-recovery.

title: Missing event-type discriminator
message: The Go call at line 1332 passes events.RemoteChangeDetected as the second argument to emitDiskChangeEvents, distinguishing remote-pull events from other disk-change sources. The Rust call passes only the pulled slice with no event-type parameter, so emitted events will either default to the wrong type or be indistinguishable from locally-detected changes, breaking consumers that filter on event type.

title: Spurious forcedRescanPaths removal
message: The Rust implementation adds a loop removing each pulled path from self.forcedRescanPaths. No corresponding logic exists in the Go updateLocalsFromPulling method (lines 1328-1334). This injected behavior may prematurely clear rescan requests that should persist until a separate code path clears them, potentially affecting scenario/folder-type-behavior tests.

title: Parameter order swapped vs Go signature
message: Go defines `Completion(device protocol.DeviceID, folder string)` (device first, folder second) but Rust defines `Completion(&self, folder_id: &str, _device: &str)` (folder first, device second). Any caller mapping positional arguments from the Go interface will silently pass the device value as the folder and vice versa, producing wrong completion results or lookup failures.

title: Device parameter ignored — per-device semantics lost
message: The Rust `_device` parameter is explicitly unused (underscore-prefixed) and the method delegates to `self.folderCompletion(folder_id)` which computes folder-level completion only. The Go implementation uses the `device` argument to compute per-device completion for a given folder, which is a fundamentally different semantic: a folder may be 100% synced with device A but only 40% synced with device B. Dropping the device dimension collapses this into a single value and breaks all parity tests that assert per-device completion (scenario/daemon-api-surface, scenario/folder-type-behavior, scenario/global-need-decision).

title: Missing error return path
message: Go returns `(FolderCompletion, error)` allowing callers to distinguish between a valid zero-completion and an error (e.g., unknown folder, database failure). Rust returns only `FolderCompletion` with no error channel, so error conditions such as a nonexistent folder will either panic or silently return a default/zero completion, which is observably different behavior on the wire (the REST API surfaces this error to clients).

title: Device type downgraded from DeviceID to plain string
message: Go uses `protocol.DeviceID` (a fixed-length byte array with validation and canonical formatting) while Rust accepts `&str`. This loses compile-time type safety and allows malformed device identifiers to reach the function without validation, which can cause divergent behavior when the API receives an invalid device ID (Go would reject at the type boundary; Rust would pass it through).

title: Missing config.Wrapper default-folder base
message: Go calls w.DefaultFolder() to obtain a base FolderConfiguration populated with user-configured defaults (e.g. default rescan interval, default ignore-permissions, versioning settings, etc.) before overwriting selected fields. Rust uses Default::default(), which yields zero/empty values for every field. Any caller relying on wrapper-supplied defaults will get a structurally different FolderConfiguration, breaking scenario/folder-type-behavior and scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

title: Missing label parameter
message: Go accepts a label string parameter and assigns it to fcfg.Label (lib/model/model.go:3459). The Rust signature omits the label parameter entirely, so the resulting FolderConfiguration.label is always an empty string. Any caller that passes a non-empty label will silently lose it, producing wire-incompatible folder metadata.

title: Missing fsType parameter
message: Go accepts an fsType config.FilesystemType parameter and assigns it to fcfg.FilesystemType (lib/model/model.go:3460). The Rust signature omits this parameter, so FilesystemType always takes its Default value. If Default does not match the caller-intended filesystem type (e.g. FilesystemTypeFake for tests), the folder will operate on the wrong filesystem abstraction, affecting scenario/folder-type-behavior and scenario/crash-recovery.

title: Need detection uses sequence instead of version comparison
message: Rust `requires_update` determines need by comparing `global.sequence > current.sequence` or deleted-status divergence, whereas Go `needSizeRemote` at `internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_counts.go:79` determines need by checking whether the device possesses a file with the same `version_idx` as the global winner (`f.version_idx = g.version_idx`). Version vectors and sequences are distinct concepts in Syncthing; two files can share a sequence ordering yet differ in version, or vice versa. This will produce wrong need counts in any scenario where a device holds a conflicting or concurrent version of a file, or where sequence values do not monotonically track version-vector changes.

title: Missing local vs remote device dispatch
message: Go `CountNeed` at `internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_counts.go:34` branches on `device == protocol.LocalDeviceID`, routing local-device queries to `needSizeLocal()` which reads precomputed aggregate counts from the `counts` table filtered by `FlagLocalNeeded`, and remote-device queries to `needSizeRemote()` which runs a SQL UNION ALL against the `files` table. Rust `count_need` at db.rs:1065 has a single code path that iterates all global winners via `for_each_global_winner` regardless of device identity. The Go local path leverages incrementally-maintained counts that reflect `LocalFlags` metadata; the Rust path recomputes from scratch and has no awareness of `LocalDeviceID` as a sentinel, so it may also diverge if the local device's entry in the store uses a different string representation than expected.

title: Counts struct missing Symlinks field
message: Go `Counts` at `internal/db/counts.go:18` includes a `Symlinks int` field tracked separately from files and directories; `TotalItems()` at line 40 sums `Files + Directories + Symlinks + Deleted`. Rust `Counts` at db.rs:81 has no symlinks field. In `add_file_to_counts` at db.rs:1396, a symlink that is not a directory will fall into the `else` branch and increment `files`, misclassifying symlinks as regular files and producing incorrect counts for any folder containing symlinks.

title: Counts struct missing Sequence, DeviceID, LocalFlags metadata
message: Go `Counts` at `internal/db/counts.go:16` carries `Sequence int64`, `DeviceID protocol.DeviceID`, and `LocalFlags protocol.FlagLocal` fields that are populated by `summarizeCounts` and consumed downstream (e.g., the `Add` method at line 27 merges `LocalFlags` via bitwise OR). Rust `Counts` omits all three fields, meaning any caller that depends on per-bucket metadata from `count_need` results—such as distinguishing counts by local-flag category—will receive incomplete data. The Rust struct also adds a `receive_only_changed usize` field with no Go counterpart, introducing schema drift.

title: String output format mismatch
message: Go's `EnumStringOf` returns the bare canonical protobuf enum name (e.g. `MESSAGE_TYPE_CLUSTER_CONFIG`) by looking up the enum descriptor, while the Rust implementation delegates to `as_json_string(self)` which produces a JSON-serialized representation; at minimum the JSON path wraps the value in double quotes and may emit a numeric value or a differently-cased variant name depending on serde annotations, so the two implementations will never produce byte-identical output for any input, breaking wire-log compatibility and any downstream consumer that pattern-matches on the string form.

title: Symbol/code mismatch in mapping
message: The mapping metadata declares the Go symbol as `FileDownloadProgressUpdate.String` (a message-level method whose generated `String()` uses `prototext.Format` to emit protobuf text format), but the Go snippet provided is `MessageType.String()` (an enum-level method using `EnumStringOf`); if the Rust `as_json_string` implementation is intended to cover the message-type `String()`, the divergence is even wider because protobuf text format differs structurally from JSON, and if it is intended to cover the enum-type `String()`, the mapping metadata is wrong, risking silent coverage gaps in the required parity tests.

title: Return-type divergence
message: The Go method `ProtoMessage()` is a no-op marker with no return value (unit/void), existing solely to satisfy the `proto.Message` interface. The Rust port changes the signature to return `bool` (always `true`), introducing a fabricated return value. Any downstream Rust code that branches on this boolean is exercising a code path with no Go equivalent, breaking semantic equivalence and type fidelity.

title: Symbol-receiver mismatch
message: The mapping metadata declares the Go symbol as `Index.ProtoMessage`, but the provided Go source at line 458 shows the receiver is `*Hello`, not `*Index`. If the Rust implementation was ported against the `Hello` struct while the mapping targets `Index`, the wrong source was used; if `Index.ProtoMessage` exists elsewhere in the file with identical shape this is a documentation error, but either way the mapping is unverifiable as presented.

title: Local device not excluded
message: Go's ListDevicesForFolder (internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_local.go:200) explicitly excludes the local device via the SQL predicate "s.device_idx != {{.LocalDeviceIdx}}", ensuring only remote peers are returned. The Rust implementation at db.rs:1025-1027 delegates to Store::devices_in_folder (store.rs:395-400) which returns all device keys from the BTreeMap with no local-device filtering. There is no local_device_id field on Store or WalFreeDb, and no grep hits for "local_device" in store.rs. Callers that iterate the result to enumerate remote peers (e.g., RemoteSequences at db.rs:490, DropDevice at db.rs:994) will incorrectly include the local device, breaking index-sequence and global-need-decision scenarios.

title: Missing device parameter
message: The Go interface signature `DismissPendingFolder(device protocol.DeviceID, folder string) error` accepts both a device ID and a folder string, meaning it dismisses a pending folder offer from a specific device. The Rust implementation `DismissPendingFolder(&mut self, folder: &str)` omits the `device` parameter entirely, making it impossible to dismiss per-device pending folder entries and breaking the daemon API surface contract.

title: Wrong data structure operated on
message: The Rust implementation removes from `self.foldersRunning`, which tracks actively running folders, not pending folder offers. The Go method `DismissPendingFolder` is expected to remove entries from the pending-folders observation store (typically `db.ObservedDevice`/`db.PendingFolder` records keyed by device+folder). Operating on the wrong collection means the method neither dismisses the pending offer nor preserves running folder state, causing both a missing side-effect and a destructive unintended side-effect.

title: Missing error return
message: The Go interface returns `error`, allowing callers to detect and handle failures (e.g., database errors when removing the pending record). The Rust function returns unit `()`, silently discarding any failure. This breaks callers that rely on error propagation, including the REST API layer which maps this error to HTTP status codes.

title: Return type not semantically equivalent
message: Go ProtoReflect returns protoreflect.Message, a live reflection interface that exposes Descriptor(), Get(), Set(), Range(), NewField(), and other field-level introspection methods used by the protobuf runtime for marshalling, merging, and wire-format negotiation. The Rust port returns serde_json::Value, a static JSON snapshot that cannot support any of these operations. Any future caller (or parity test such as protocol-state-transition) that exercises reflection-based marshalling or descriptor traversal will get structurally incompatible behavior.

title: Nil receiver path missing
message: Go lines 462-469 handle a nil receiver by falling through to mi.MessageOf(x), which returns a valid zero-value protoreflect.Message carrying the message descriptor. This is part of the protobuf API contract and is used by generic code that inspects message types without an instance. The Rust signature takes &self, making this path unreachable; an Option::None would need to be handled at the call site, but no such handling exists.

title: Silent null on serialization failure
message: as_value uses unwrap_or(Value::Null), silently returning JSON null if serde serialization fails. Go's ProtoReflect is infallible—it wraps the existing message pointer in a reflection adapter and never produces an error. A serialization failure in Rust (e.g., from a future field type that does not implement Serialize) would produce Value::Null instead of surfacing the error, masking data loss in crash-recovery or index-sequence-behavior scenarios.

title: Deleted entries double-counted
message: Go's summarizeCounts (internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_counts.go:27-39) uses a switch statement where the r.Deleted case is mutually exclusive with file-type cases, so deleted entries only increment c.Deleted without touching Files, Directories, or Bytes. Rust's add_file_to_counts (syncthing-rs/src/db.rs:1398-1407) uses independent if-statements, so a deleted file increments both counts.deleted AND counts.files (or counts.directories), and unconditionally adds file.size to counts.bytes. This produces inflated files/directories/bytes counts whenever deleted entries exist in the global state.

title: Symlinks misclassified as files
message: Go's summarizeCounts (internal/db/sqlite/folderdb_counts.go:36-38) has an explicit case for FileInfoTypeSymlink that increments c.Symlinks and adds to c.Bytes. Rust's add_file_to_counts has no symlink branch; the else clause at line 1404 causes symlinks to be counted as files, inflating the files count and losing symlink tracking entirely.

title: Counts struct missing fields
message: Go's Counts struct (internal/db/counts.go:16-25) includes Symlinks int, Sequence int64, DeviceID protocol.DeviceID, and LocalFlags protocol.FlagLocal. Rust's Counts struct omits all four. Go's summarizeCounts sets DeviceID to protocol.LocalDeviceID on every CountGlobal result; any consumer relying on these fields (e.g., the Sequence or DeviceID for display or protocol serialization) will receive default/zero values from Rust, breaking schema compatibility.

title: Receiver type mismatch Index vs IndexUpdate
message: The mapping specifies Go symbol `IndexUpdate.GetFiles` but the provided Go source at line 859 defines the method on `*Index`, not `*IndexUpdate`. If the Rust implementation attaches `GetFiles` to an `IndexUpdate` struct rather than an `Index` struct, callers invoking it on the wrong type will silently return unrelated data or fail to compile against correct call sites. Verify which struct the Rust method is implemented on and ensure it matches the intended Go receiver.

title: Clone semantics diverge from shared-slice return
message: The Go method returns the internal `x.Files` slice directly, meaning the caller holds a reference into the receiver's owned data and mutations to elements (e.g., `ret[0].Name = "x"`) propagate back to the struct. The Rust method returns `self.Files.clone()`, producing a fully independent `Vec<FileInfo>`. Any downstream code path that relies on mutating the returned collection and observing the effect through the original struct (or vice versa) will behave differently. This matters for crash-recovery and index-sequence-behavior scenarios where in-place patching of file entries may occur.

title: Pointer-element vs value-element type fidelity
message: Go returns `[]*FileInfo` (slice of pointers), preserving identity: two slices can share the same `*FileInfo` and observe each other's mutations. Rust returns `Vec<FileInfo>` (owned values), so element identity is lost after the clone. If any protocol-state-transition or index-sequence-behavior test compares element identity or relies on pointer aliasing between the struct's field and the returned slice elements, it will not replicate correctly.

title: Nil-receiver path has no Rust equivalent
message: Go's nil-receiver guard (lines 860-863) returns a nil slice when the receiver pointer is nil. Rust's `&self` is guaranteed non-null, so this path is structurally absent. While Rust's type system prevents the nil-receiver case in safe code, any FFI or unsafe caller that could produce an analogous null state would trigger undefined behavior instead of the graceful nil-return the Go code provides.

title: Return type not equivalent
message: Go Descriptor() returns protoreflect.EnumDescriptor, a rich interface exposing FullName(), Values(), Options(), and other reflection methods used by protobuf runtime and serialization infrastructure; Rust returns &'static str, a plain string with none of those capabilities, so any consumer relying on descriptor introspection (e.g. protobuf JSON marshalling, reflection-based routing, or the required parity tests that exercise protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior) will receive an incompatible type and either fail to compile against the expected trait bounds or silently lose descriptor metadata.

title: Symbol-kind mismatch in macro
message: The review target maps IndexUpdate.Descriptor (a message-level descriptor returning protoreflect.MessageDescriptor) but the Go snippet provided is MessageType.Descriptor (an enum-level descriptor returning protoreflect.EnumDescriptor via file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes[0]); the Rust macro uses a single descriptor($name) path for both, collapsing the distinction between EnumDescriptor and MessageDescriptor into one &'static str, which means the message variant and enum variant are not differentiated on the Rust side even though they carry structurally different protobuf reflection data in Go.

title: Visibility narrower than Go
message: Go Descriptor() is an exported public method (uppercase initial) satisfying the protoreflect.Enum interface and callable by any external package; Rust declares the method pub(crate), restricting it to crate-internal use, which will prevent external consumers or integration-test crates (including the required scenario/crash-recovery and scenario/protocol-state-transition parity tests if they live outside the crate) from invoking it.

title: Return type mismatch: EnumType vs string
message: The Go method returns `protoreflect.EnumType` (a rich reflection interface backed by `&file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes[0]`), which exposes methods such as `Descriptor()`, `New(EnumNumber)`, and full protobuf enum reflection capabilities. The Rust port returns `&'static str` (a plain string name), collapsing the entire reflection surface to a single scalar. Any downstream code that calls `.Descriptor()` or `.New()` on the returned type—including protocol-state-transition or index-sequence-behavior scenarios that dynamically resolve enum values from wire numbers—will have no equivalent path in the Rust implementation, breaking wire/schema compatibility for round-trip enum resolution.

title: Receiver semantics: value vs reference
message: The Go method uses a value receiver `(MessageType)` and ignores the receiver entirely, always returning the static type table entry for index 0. The Rust method takes `&self` and returns `$name`, a macro-injected string literal. While both are effectively static, the Go version is bound to a specific index in `file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes` which ties it to code-generation order; the Rust version relies on a macro parameter with no equivalent index-based linkage, meaning a code-generation reorder in Go would silently diverge from the Rust mapping with no compile-time detection.

title: Go symbol vs snippet mismatch
message: The mapping declares the Go symbol as `FileDownloadProgressUpdateType.Type` but the provided Go snippet shows the method defined on `MessageType` (line 74). If these are distinct generated enum types that share the same code pattern but point to different indices in `file_bep_bep_proto_enumTypes` (index 0 vs another index), the Rust macro expansion must emit the correct per-enum-type identity; a single `$name` string cannot distinguish which protobuf enum type descriptor is being referenced, risking incorrect type identity when multiple enum types coexist.

title: Lazy iterator replaced with eager Vec
message: Go returns (iter.Seq[protocol.FileInfo], func() error) at interface.go:39, providing a lazy pull-based iterator that streams FileInfo values one at a time and a separate closure to retrieve any error that occurred during iteration. Rust returns Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String> at db.rs:153, which eagerly materializes the entire result set into memory before returning. This breaks streaming semantics, changes memory profile (directly relevant to the scenario/memory-cap-50mb parity test), and alters error observability: Go callers can consume partial results before discovering a mid-iteration error via the closure, whereas Rust callers receive either all results or an error atomically with no partial-consumption path.

title: DeviceID downgraded to plain string
message: Go's AllLocalFiles accepts protocol.DeviceID (a strongly-typed 32-byte binary identifier with its own marshaling, validation, and equality semantics) at interface.go:39. Rust accepts &str at db.rs:153, losing binary type fidelity, compile-time type safety, and any validation that DeviceID construction enforces. Callers can pass arbitrary strings that would be rejected by the Go type system, and wire/schema compatibility with the Syncthing BEP protocol is broken because DeviceID is a fixed-size binary value, not a variable-length UTF-8 string.

title: Two-phase error model collapsed to single Result
message: Go's signature at interface.go:39 returns a func() error closure that is called after iteration completes to retrieve deferred errors (e.g., storage I/O errors encountered mid-scan). This allows callers to distinguish between "no results because empty" and "no results because of error" after partial consumption. Rust's Result<Vec<FileInfo>, String> at db.rs:153 collapses this into a single up-front error check, eliminating the ability to handle late-arising errors distinctly from early ones and changing the observable error-handling contract that callers of the Go interface depend on.

title: Error-path collapse loses paused/not-running distinction
message: Go's checkFolderRunningRLocked (lib/model/model.go:3243-3256) returns three distinct sentinel errors depending on folder state: ErrFolderMissing ("no such folder") when the folder config does not exist, ErrFolderPaused ("folder is paused") when the config exists but is paused, or ErrFolderNotRunning ("folder is not running") when the config exists and is not paused but the runner is absent. The Rust FolderErrors implementation only checks folderRunners.get(folder_id) and returns ErrFolderMissing for any absent key, collapsing all three error cases into one. API consumers that branch on error type (e.g., lib/api/api.go:903 checks errors.Is(err, ErrFolderPaused)) will receive incorrect errors and take wrong code paths.

title: ErrFolderMissing string drift
message: Go defines ErrFolderMissing as "no such folder" (lib/model/model.go:195) while Rust defines it as "folder missing" (model_core.rs:19). Any client or test that matches on this error string for API compatibility or error-type discrimination will see a different value, breaking wire-level behavioral equivalence.

title: Returned errors not sorted by path
message: Go's folder.Errors() (lib/model/folder.go:1302-1304) sorts the merged error slice by Path using slices.SortFunc with strings.Compare, producing deterministic alphabetical ordering in the REST API response. Rust's folder::Errors() returns self.errorsMut.clone() with no sorting, so the API response order is insertion-order-dependent and will differ from Go's output, breaking scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

title: Pull errors from tempPullErrors never merged into Errors output
message: Go's folder.Errors() (lib/model/folder.go:1298-1301) merges both scanErrors and pullErrors into a single returned slice. In Rust, the folder struct has only errorsMut (folder_core.rs:363) with no separate pullErrors field. The sendReceiveFolder struct accumulates pull errors in tempPullErrors (folder_core.rs:1648) during copierRoutine, but these are never transferred into folder.errorsMut. Since folder::Errors() (folder_core.rs:483-484) only returns errorsMut.clone(), pull errors routed through tempPullErrors are silently dropped from the FolderErrors response, causing data loss visible to the REST API and event system.

title: Missing subDirs parameter
message: The Go method accepts `subDirs []string` and uses it to selectively clear only those scan errors whose path matches or is a parent of a requested subdirectory. The Rust method takes no parameters at all, making selective clearing impossible and forcing every call to behave as an unconditional bulk operation, which breaks callers that pass a non-empty subdirectory list.

title: Wrong filtering predicate
message: Go removes entries where `fe.Path == sub
column7: fs.IsParent(fe.Path, sub)` — a filesystem-path-based match against the requested subdirectories. Rust instead removes entries where `e.Err.contains("scan")` — a substring match on the error message text. These are entirely unrelated predicates; the Rust version will incorrectly drop errors unrelated to requested paths and retain errors that should be cleared, producing wrong state after every selective clear.

title: errorsMut used as collection instead of mutex
message: In Go, `errorsMut` is a `sync.Mutex` that guards the separate `scanErrors []FileError` slice. In Rust, `errorsMut` is treated as the error collection itself (`.retain()` is called on it). This conflates the lock with the data it protects, meaning either the field was mistyped or a separate `scan_errors` collection is missing entirely, resulting in incorrect data-model structure and lost mutual-exclusion semantics if other code accesses scan errors through a different field.

title: Missing empty-subDirs fast path
message: Go has an explicit branch: when `subDirs` is empty, it sets `f.scanErrors = nil`, unconditionally clearing all errors and returning early. The Rust implementation has no equivalent branch. Even if the parameter were added, the current code would iterate and filter rather than bulk-clear, diverging from Go behavior where an empty-subDirs call is the "clear everything" signal used after full scans.

title: Eager Vec replaces lazy iterator
message: Go returns `(iter.Seq[BlockMapEntry], func() error)` — a lazy, streaming iterator that yields entries on demand without materializing all results in memory. Rust returns `Result<Vec<BlockMapEntry>, String>`, which eagerly collects every matching block-map entry into a single allocation before the caller sees any data. This breaks streaming/early-termination semantics and violates the memory-cap-50mb parity scenario: a folder with millions of blocks sharing a hash will OOM in Rust where Go would stream them incrementally.

title: Deferred error callback lost
message: Go's second return value `func() error` is a deferred error reporter: iteration can begin successfully and the error surfaces only after the iterator is drained or abandoned, allowing partial consumption of valid results before an I/O error is detected. Rust's `Result<Vec<BlockMapEntry>, String>` is fail-fast — any storage error prevents returning any entries at all. This changes observable behavior in crash-recovery and WAL-free-durability scenarios where a partial read is valid and expected, and means callers cannot distinguish "zero results, no error" from "error before any results" in the same way the Go API does.

title: Vec-to-Set data-structure change drops duplicates
message: Go appends to a slice (`append(b.toRemove, item)`), preserving duplicates; Rust inserts into a set (`self.toRemove.insert(…)`), which silently deduplicates. If `Remove` is called twice with the same path, Go's `toRemove` contains two entries and downstream iteration processes the path twice, whereas Rust's contains one. Any consumer that relies on duplicate presence (e.g., counting removals, or idempotency guards that expect to see every call reflected) will behave differently.

title: Insertion-order vs set-order iteration divergence
message: Go's slice guarantees iteration in insertion order. Rust's `HashSet` yields an arbitrary iteration order (or `BTreeSet` yields lexicographic order). If any downstream consumer of `toRemove` is order-sensitive—such as logging, batched database operations, or conflict resolution that depends on first-seen ordering—the port will produce different results under identical inputs.

title: Partial-failure leaves inconsistent state
message: In Go, DropFolder atomically removes the folder metadata row first (DELETE FROM folders, db_update.go:27-30), so even if subsequent file removal fails the folder is logically gone from all queries (ListFolders, GetIndexID, etc.). In Rust, drop_all_files iterates devices and deletes files one-by-one via journal appends; if any delete_file call fails (e.g., IO error on journal append), the early return via ? at line 991 leaves index_ids and mtimes intact while file data for previously-iterated devices is already deleted, producing an inconsistent half-dropped folder with no recovery path. A retry of drop_folder would find fewer devices (some already cleaned from the files map) but index_ids/mtimes still reference the folder.

title: No crash-recovery cleanup for orphaned metadata
message: Go provides cleanDroppedFolders (db_update.go:52-79) as a startup safety net that removes orphaned folder database files after a crash during DropFolder. Rust has no equivalent: if the process crashes after journal-appended file deletions complete in the drop_all_files loop (line 990-991) but before persist_runtime_metadata executes at line 995, on next startup journal replay will delete the files but the runtime_metadata.json still contains index_ids and mtimes entries for the dropped folder, causing stale index ID lookups (get_index_id would return a valid ID for a folder with no files) while Go would have already removed the metadata row and swept orphaned data on restart.

title: device param type mismatch: DeviceID vs &str
message: Go `CountLocal` accepts `device protocol.DeviceID` which is a `[32]byte` fixed-size typed wrapper with built-in validation, canonical string encoding (luhn-checked base32), and binary identity semantics. The Rust `count_local` accepts `device: &str`, a plain UTF-8 string with no compile-time or runtime enforcement of valid device identity format. This permits callers to pass malformed or non-canonical device identifiers that Go would reject at the type boundary, risking silent lookup misses (no matching device rows) or inconsistent key construction in the storage layer, which would cause divergent results between the Go and Rust implementations for the same logical device.

title: Missing DropFilesNamed database side-effect
message: The Go method calls `b.f.db.DropFilesNamed(b.f.folderID, protocol.LocalDeviceID, b.toRemove)` to persist the removal of file entries in the database before clearing the batch. The Rust method performs no database operation at all; it merely clones the pending removals into a return vector and clears the internal list. Even if the caller is expected to issue the DB call, the atomicity guarantee is broken: Go ties the clear to a successful DB write, while Rust separates them unconditionally.

title: Unconditional clear loses retry-on-error semantics
message: In Go, `b.toRemove` is only reset (`b.toRemove[:0]`) after `DropFilesNamed` succeeds. If the DB call fails, the slice retains its contents so a subsequent flush can retry. The Rust version calls `self.toRemove.clear()` unconditionally with no error path, so if the downstream consumer of the returned `Vec` fails to persist the removals, the data is already lost from the batch and cannot be retried.

title: Return type mismatch breaks error contract
message: Go returns `error` (nil on success, non-nil on DB failure). Rust returns `Vec<String>`, providing no mechanism to signal failure. Callers in Go can propagate the error upward (e.g., aborting a scan); Rust callers receive a value that is always considered successful, eliminating the error-handling branch that exists in every Go call-site of `flushToRemove`.

title: Empty-input guard omitted
message: Go explicitly checks `len(b.toRemove) > 0` before performing any work, avoiding unnecessary DB calls on an empty batch. The Rust version always allocates and returns a `Vec` and calls `clear()` regardless. While functionally benign in isolation, this diverges from Go's guard pattern and would cause a spurious DB call if the DB interaction were restored, and it introduces a needless allocation of an empty `Vec` on every no-op flush.

title: Return type mismatch uint64 vs i64
message: Go `GetIndexId` returns `uint64`, but the Rust port returns `i64`. Any index ID with the high bit set (value >= 2^63) will be interpreted as a negative number in Rust, silently corrupting the value. This breaks wire/schema compatibility with the protobuf definition (`uint64 index_id = 2`) and will cause comparison mismatches during index-sequence-behavior and protocol-state-transition scenarios. The return type and the underlying `IndexId` field type must both be `u64`.

title: Lazy iterator replaced with eager Vec
message: Go returns `iter.Seq[db.FileMetadata]`, a lazy pull-based iterator that streams results one at a time without materializing the full result set in memory. The Rust port returns `Vec<db::FileMetadata>`, which eagerly collects every record into a heap-allocated vector before the caller sees any element. For folders with millions of global entries this changes the memory profile from O(1) streaming to O(n) resident, likely violating the `memory-cap-50mb` parity test and altering backpressure semantics for any caller that short-circuits iteration early.

title: Error path silently swallowed
message: Go's signature returns a second value `func() error` that lets the caller discover errors encountered during or after iteration (e.g., database I/O failure, corruption). The Rust implementation chains `.ok()` on the RwLock read (line 634) and `.ok()` on the inner `all_global_files` call (line 635), then falls back to `unwrap_or_default()` (line 636), converting every error—poisoned lock, database failure, missing folder—into an empty `Vec`. Callers cannot distinguish "folder has zero global files" from "database is unreachable," breaking error-reporting contracts required by `scenario/crash-recovery` and `scenario/daemon-api-surface`.

title: Missing error-closure return value changes API contract
message: Go's `AllGlobalFiles` returns two values `(iter.Seq[db.FileMetadata], func() error)`, where the second is a deferred error reporter typically called after iteration completes. The Rust port returns only `Vec<db::FileMetadata>` with no error channel. Any caller that relied on the error closure to commit, rollback, or log post-iteration status has no equivalent mechanism, breaking the interface contract and any upstream code that pattern-matches on the two-return-value shape.

title: Return-type mismatch: void vs bool
message: The Go method `ProtoMessage()` is a no-op marker with no return value (signature `func (*Hello) ProtoMessage() {}`), existing solely to satisfy the `proto.Message` interface as a type-level tag. The Rust port changes the signature to `fn ProtoMessage(&self) -> bool` and returns `true`, converting a zero-cost marker into a boolean predicate. Any Rust caller that branches on this return value is exercising a code path with no Go equivalent, and any future interop or trait-based dispatch that expects unit-return semantics will break. The Rust side should return `()` (or use a marker trait) to preserve semantic equivalence.

title: nil-vs-empty slice divergence
message: Go returns a nil slice when the receiver's BlockIndexes field is nil (the zero-value for an unset repeated protobuf field), and Go callers can distinguish nil from empty via `slice == nil`. The Rust implementation always returns a concrete empty `Vec<i32>` via `.clone()` on a default-initialized Vec, making it impossible for downstream logic to distinguish "field was never set" (nil) from "field was explicitly set to empty." Any Rust caller ported from Go code that branches on nil-ness (e.g., presence checks, conditional serialization, or the crash-recovery and protocol-state-transition test scenarios that may assert on field-set semantics) will observe different behavior. To preserve parity, the return type should be `Option<Vec<i32>>` with `None` representing Go's nil slice, or the field itself should be `Option<Vec<i32>>`.

title: Flush condition checks wrong quantity
message: Go checks only `len(b.toRemove) >= maxToRemove` (line 620) to decide whether to flush removes, using a dedicated constant threshold applied solely to the remove queue. Rust checks the combined sum `self.updateBatch.len() + self.toRemove.len() >= threshold`, which conflates the two independent batch sizes into a single comparison. This means a large updateBatch with an empty toRemove can trigger a flush in Rust but never would in Go at this check, and conversely a full toRemove queue paired with a small updateBatch may fail to trigger when it should.

title: Two-stage independent flush collapsed into single all-or-nothing flush
message: Go performs two independent flush decisions: first it conditionally flushes only the remove queue via `flushToRemove()` (line 621), then unconditionally delegates to `updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` (line 625) which applies its own separate threshold to the update batch. Rust replaces this with a single `self.Flush()` that drains both queues atomically or not at all. This loses the Go behavior where the update batch can be flushed independently even when the remove queue is below threshold, and where removes can be flushed while the update batch is retained.

title: Hardcoded constant replaced with caller-supplied parameter
message: Go uses the package-level constant `maxToRemove` for the remove-queue threshold and `updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` uses its own internal constant. Rust exposes `threshold` as a caller-supplied `usize` parameter, shifting responsibility for providing the correct value to every call site and removing the guarantee that the canonical constant is used consistently.

title: Update batch unconditional flush-check path missing
message: In Go, `b.updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` on line 625 is always called regardless of whether the remove queue was flushed, meaning the update batch is checked and potentially flushed on every invocation. In Rust, when the combined-size condition on line 289 is false, `None` is returned and no flush check of the update batch occurs at all, so a full update batch will never be flushed until the combined total also exceeds the threshold.

title: Missing initial-scan-finished signaling
message: The Go implementation checks whether the `initialScanFinished` channel has already been closed via a non-blocking select; if it has not, it logs either the scan error or a success message and then closes the channel, ensuring downstream consumers (e.g., folder-start waiters) are unblocked exactly once. The Rust implementation contains no equivalent flag, atomic, or oneshot-channel close, so the initial-scan-finished event is never signaled, breaking any component that waits on it.

title: Missing Reschedule call
message: The Go function unconditionally calls `f.Reschedule()` at line 1029 after scanning, which resets the periodic scan timer. The Rust function has no corresponding reschedule invocation on any code path, so after the first timer firing, periodic scans will not be re-armed.

title: Missing error return and propagation
message: The Go function signature returns `error` (line 1015) and propagates the result of `scanSubdirs` to its caller, which uses it for health reporting. The Rust function returns `()`, discarding any scan failure. Callers cannot observe or react to scan errors, breaking crash-recovery and health-check semantics.

title: Spurious forced-rescan branching not present in Go
message: The Rust implementation introduces a branch on `forcedRescanPaths.is_empty()` that dispatches to either `Scan` or `handleForcedRescans`. The Go `scanTimerFired` has no such branching; it unconditionally calls `scanSubdirs(ctx, nil)`. Forced-rescan handling in Go is managed elsewhere (e.g., in the folder loop or `SchedulePull`). This changes the method's contract and may cause forced rescans to be consumed at the wrong point in the lifecycle.

title: Missing context/cancellation parameter
message: The Go function accepts a `context.Context` (line 1015) and passes it to `scanSubdirs` and the logger, enabling cooperative cancellation during long scans. The Rust function takes only `&mut self`, so there is no mechanism for the caller to cancel an in-progress scan, which is required for clean shutdown and folder-pause semantics tested by scenario/crash-recovery.

title: Scan invocation argument mismatch
message: Go passes `nil` to `scanSubdirs` (line 1016), semantically meaning "scan everything with no subdirectory filter." Rust passes `&[]` (an empty slice) to `Scan`. If `Scan` treats an empty slice differently from a None/absent value (e.g., scanning nothing vs. scanning everything), this is a behavioral divergence. Verify that `Scan(&[])` is semantically equivalent to `scanSubdirs(ctx, nil)`.

title: Global winner selection uses sequence heuristic instead of version vectors
message: Go selects the global file winner via fileRow.Compare (folderdb_update.go:473-510), which compares protocol version vectors (Greater/Lesser/Equal/ConcurrentGreater/ConcurrentLesser) with invalid-status demotion and device-index tiebreakers. Rust's prefer_global (db.rs:1351-1362) uses an entirely different heuristic: highest sequence, then non-deleted wins, then highest modified_ns, then lexicographic path. For multi-device scenarios with concurrent or conflicting version vectors, the two implementations will select different global winners, producing divergent results from all_global_files. This will fail scenario/global-need-decision and scenario/crash-recovery parity tests.

title: Eager Vec materialization instead of lazy streaming iterator
message: Go returns (iter.Seq[FileMetadata], func() error) which lazily streams rows one at a time from SQL with deferred error reporting and early-termination support via yield returning false (interface.go:37, folderdb_global.go:77-88, util.go:26-51). Rust returns Result<Vec<FileMetadata>, String> which eagerly materializes all results into a Vec before returning (db.rs:147, db.rs:693-701). This eliminates streaming/early-break semantics and forces full memory allocation proportional to result set size, which will fail the scenario/memory-cap-50mb parity test for large folders.

title: All-ignored files silently omitted instead of falling back to first entry
message: Go's recalcGlobalForFile (folderdb_update.go:392-397) always selects a global winner: it uses slices.IndexFunc to find the first non-invalid entry, and if all entries are invalid (globIdx < 0), it falls back to index 0 so the file is always represented in global results. Rust's best_global_candidate (db.rs:1321-1323) unconditionally skips entries where meta.ignored is true and returns None if all candidates are ignored, causing the file to be silently dropped from all_global_files output. This is an observable behavioral difference for files where every device version is marked ignored/invalid.

title: local_flags bit positions in FileMetadata methods do not match Go protocol constants
message: Go's FileMetadata methods use protocol-defined flag values: IsReceiveOnlyChanged checks FlagLocalReceiveOnly=1<<3 (bep_fileinfo.go:30), IsInvalid checks composite LocalInvalidFlags=1
column11: 8=11 (bep_fileinfo.go:41). Rust db.rs defines simplified single-bit constants FLAG_LOCAL_RECEIVE_ONLY=1<<0, FLAG_LOCAL_INVALID=1<<1, FLAG_LOCAL_CONFLICT=1<<2 (db.rs:14-16) used in the corresponding methods (db.rs:64-78). If local_flags values flow from BEP wire format or Go-compatible storage, bit 1 in Go means FlagLocalIgnored while in Rust it means FLAG_LOCAL_INVALID, causing is_receive_only_changed(), is_invalid(), and should_conflict() to all return incorrect results on returned FileMetadata.

title: Path iteration order differs due to PATH_SEG_SEP encoding
message: Go iterates global files using SQL ORDER BY n.name (folderdb_global.go:82), which sorts by raw path string where '/' is byte 0x2F. Rust uses compare_path_order (store.rs:1387-1388) via for_each_global_winner (db.rs:540), which compares encode_path_key outputs where each path segment is prefixed with PATH_SEG_SEP='\u{001e}' (byte 0x1E, store.rs:22). This changes the sort: Go orders "a-b" before "a/b" (0x2D < 0x2F), but Rust orders "a/b" before "a-b" because encoded "\x1ea\x1eb" < "\x1ea-b" (0x1E < 0x2D). Any path set containing siblings and sub-paths with characters in the 0x1E-0x2E range will produce different iteration order, failing scenario/path-order-invariant.

title: Missing NativeFilename transformation on returned names
message: Go applies osutil.NativeFilename() to each returned FileMetadata.Name (folderdb_global.go:85), which on Windows converts forward slashes to OS-native backslashes. Rust's all_global_files (db.rs:693-701) returns names as-is from the store with no platform-specific path transformation, producing forward-slash paths on Windows where Go would produce backslash paths.

title: Wrong field written
message: Go sets `file.InodeChangeNs` (inode change time / ctime) but Rust sets `fi.modified_ns` (modification time / mtime). These are semantically different filesystem timestamps; writing to `modified_ns` corrupts the modification time and leaves the inode-change-time field unset, breaking any downstream logic that depends on either field (e.g., conflict detection, database comparison).

title: Missing Lstat and error path
message: Go performs `f.mtimefs.Lstat(file.Name)` internally to obtain the current on-disk inode change time and propagates the Lstat error to the caller. Rust accepts a pre-resolved `ts_ns` parameter and has no error return (`()` instead of `Result`). Unless every call-site in the Rust port replicates the Lstat call, error check, and early-return-on-error before invoking this function, the error-propagation contract of Go is lost and callers may proceed with stale or invalid timestamps on I/O failure.

title: Missing zero-time guard
message: Go checks `!ct.IsZero()` and explicitly sets `InodeChangeNs = 0` when the platform does not support inode change time (zero value). Rust unconditionally assigns `ts_ns` with no zero-check, so a zero or negative sentinel value from the caller will be stored verbatim rather than being normalized, potentially producing different behavior on platforms that do not report ctime (e.g., some Windows or Plan 9 filesystems).

title: Scan side-effect omitted
message: Go calls f.Scan(scanList) at line 1847 to actually schedule the filesystem scan after pulling; the Rust method only returns a Vec<String> without invoking any scan operation, so the core side-effect of this routine is absent unless every call-site independently performs the scan, which is not enforced by the API contract.

title: Deduplication missing
message: Go inserts every received path into a map[string]struct{} (line 1835-1838) to deduplicate before scanning; Rust collects self.queue verbatim via iter().cloned().collect(). If self.queue is a Vec or any ordered collection that admits duplicates, the same path can appear multiple times in the returned list, causing redundant scan work and divergent behavior from Go.

title: Channel aggregation absent
message: Go's pullScannerRoutine blocks on a channel (line 1837) and aggregates all paths sent during the entire pull phase until the channel is closed; the Rust version performs no channel consumption or temporal aggregation—it snapshots self.queue at a single point in time, so paths arriving after the call but before pull completion are silently lost.

title: Queue not drained after collection
message: In Go, the map is function-local and the channel is fully consumed, so no residual state remains after the routine returns. The Rust method reads self.queue but does not clear or drain it, meaning subsequent calls would return stale/duplicate entries unless the caller explicitly clears the queue, risking double-scanning.

title: Empty-set guard missing
message: Go checks len(toBeScanned) != 0 (line 1841) and skips the scan entirely when no paths were received; Rust unconditionally returns whatever self.queue contains, including an empty Vec, which may lead callers to invoke a no-op scan if they do not replicate this guard.

title: Debug logging absent
message: Go emits a per-path debug log "Scheduling scan after pulling" via slog.DebugContext (line 1844) for observability during pull operations; the Rust implementation has no logging, reducing diagnostic parity in production troubleshooting.

title: String output format diverges from protobuf enum name
message: The Go `FileDownloadProgressUpdateType.String()` at `internal/gen/bep/bep.pb.go:414-415` delegates to `protoimpl.X.EnumStringOf` which resolves the enum descriptor and returns the protobuf enum value name (e.g. `"FILE_DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS_UPDATE_TYPE_APPEND"` for 0, `"FILE_DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS_UPDATE_TYPE_FORGET"` for 1). The Rust implementation uses the `impl_enumish!` macro (line 158-161 instantiation), whose `String()` at line 145-147 returns `format!("{}({})", $name, self.Number())`, producing `"FileDownloadProgressUpdateType(0)"` and `"FileDownloadProgressUpdateType(1)"` respectively. These are entirely different string representations, breaking any log parsing, wire-format compatibility, or equality comparisons against the Go output. Note also that the review-target snippet (lines 43-45, `as_json_string`) is from `impl_proto_common!` which applies to message types, not to `FileDownloadProgressUpdateType`; the actual enum path is `impl_enumish!`.

title: Unknown enum value stringification differs
message: For out-of-range enum values (e.g. integer 99), Go's `EnumStringOf` returns just the numeric string `"99"`, while the Rust `impl_enumish!` macro produces `"FileDownloadProgressUpdateType(99)"`. Any consumer that falls back to parsing the numeric string for unrecognized values will fail against the Rust output.

title: Signature changes single path to slice
message: Go ScheduleForceRescan accepts a single path string and unconditionally inserts exactly one entry into forcedRescanPaths (folder.go:1309-1311). The Rust port changes the signature to accept &[String], allowing zero or many paths. This enables callers like scanOnWatchErr (folder_core.rs:878) to invoke ScheduleForceRescan(&[]) which inserts nothing into forcedRescanPaths yet still sets scanScheduled=true, a call pattern impossible in Go where the equivalent scanOnWatchErr (folder.go:1216) calls the distinct method DelayScan(0) instead.

title: Model-level bypasses folder ScheduleForceRescan
message: In Go, model.recheckFile calls runner.ScheduleForceRescan(name) (model.go:2151) which invokes the folder method that records the path in forcedRescanPaths and non-blocking-signals the forcedRescanRequested channel. In Rust, model_core::ScheduleForceRescan (model_core.rs:1360-1366) delegates to self.Scan() which calls ScanFolderSubdirs (model_core.rs:1353-1358) and then folder.Scan(subs) (model_core.rs:907), completely bypassing folder_core::ScheduleForceRescan. The path is never inserted into forcedRescanPaths via this call path, so the file is not marked for forced rehash on the next scan, losing the core behavioral intent of the method.

title: Forced-rescan signal conflated with regular scan flag
message: Go uses a dedicated forcedRescanRequested channel (folder.go:80,1314-1316) with non-blocking send semantics, giving the folder serve loop a distinct case to differentiate forced-rescan triggers from regular scan scheduling. The Rust port replaces this with forcedRescanRequested() simply setting self.scanScheduled = true (folder_core.rs:595), the same boolean flag used by ScheduleScan (folder_core.rs:532). This merges the two signal paths, preventing any downstream logic from distinguishing a forced-rescan request from a regular scan request.

title: device param type mismatch
message: Go `GetDeviceFile` accepts `device protocol.DeviceID`, a strongly-typed 32-byte identifier with specific encoding, comparison, and marshaling semantics, but Rust `get_device_file` accepts `device: &str`, a plain string reference. The Rust codebase already defines a `DeviceId` type used in return positions (e.g., lines 144, 199), so the input parameter should be `&DeviceId` rather than `&str` to preserve type safety and ensure binary-safe device identity comparison; accepting an arbitrary `&str` risks encoding mismatches and bypasses validation that `protocol.DeviceID` enforces in Go.

title: Completely different function signature and semantics
message: The Go function takes a single slice of protocol.FileInfo and persists them to the local database via f.db.Update(folderID, LocalDeviceID, fs), then queries the resulting sequence number. The Rust function instead takes two slices (local and remote) of VersionedFile and calls compute_need(local, remote), which is an entirely different operation — it computes a diff/need-set rather than updating local file metadata in the database. The inputs, core logic, and purpose are not equivalent.

title: Missing database persistence
message: The Go function calls f.db.Update(f.folderID, protocol.LocalDeviceID, fs) at line 1337 to persist the updated file records to the database and returns the error if that fails. The Rust function performs no database write at all, meaning local index updates are silently lost.

title: Missing LocalIndexUpdated event emission
message: The Go function at lines 1354-1360 logs a LocalIndexUpdated event containing the folder ID, item count, filenames, sequence number, and a legacy "version" field equal to sequence. The Rust function emits no event, breaking any downstream consumer (UI, other devices, event subscriptions) that depends on this notification.

title: Missing sequence query and error path
message: The Go function at line 1350 calls f.db.GetDeviceSequence to retrieve the current local sequence number after the update, and returns an error if that call fails. The Rust function has no equivalent query and no error return at all (it returns Vec<String> instead of Result), eliminating two distinct error-propagation paths present in the Go code.

title: Wrong items removed from forcedRescanPaths
message: The Go function iterates over the input FileInfo slice and removes each file.Name from forcedRescanPaths (lines 1343-1347), i.e., it removes the names of files that were just updated locally. The Rust function instead removes need.need_paths — the output of a need computation between local and remote — which is a different set of paths. This changes which files are eligible for forced rescan.

title: Return type incompatibility
message: The Go function returns error (nil on success). The Rust function returns Vec<String> containing computed need paths. No caller of the Go function expects a list of needed paths back; this return value has no equivalent in the original API contract and suggests the Rust function was mapped from a different Go method.

title: String output format mismatch: proto text vs JSON
message: Go IndexUpdate.String() at bep.pb.go:891 delegates to protoimpl.X.MessageStringOf(x) which emits protobuf text format (field:value pairs without quotes on keys, nested braces for sub-messages), whereas the Rust impl_proto_common macro at bep_compat.rs:42 delegates to as_json_string (bep_compat.rs:16-17) which calls serde_json::to_string, producing JSON with quoted keys, arrays, and a fundamentally different grammar. Any downstream code that pattern-matches, parses, or compares the String() output—including log-grepping in crash-recovery or protocol-state-transition test scenarios—will observe divergent results.

title: Nil-receiver vs no-None divergence on empty value
message: Go IndexUpdate.String() accepts a *IndexUpdate (pointer receiver); calling it on a nil pointer yields an empty string via MessageStringOf. The Rust port takes &self which cannot be None; calling String() on a Default-constructed IndexUpdate produces a non-empty JSON object with all default field values serialized (e.g. {"folder":"","files":[]}). This changes observable behavior for the empty/zero-value edge case, which may affect crash-recovery test scenarios that check log output after partial state initialization.

title: Lazy iterator replaced with eager Vec
message: Go returns (iter.Seq[FileMetadata], func() error) at internal/db/interface.go:38, providing a lazy pull-based iterator that streams results one at a time and a deferred error-closure that reports errors encountered during iteration. Rust returns Result<Vec<FileMetadata>, String> at syncthing-rs/src/db.rs:152, which eagerly materializes all matching records into memory before returning and collapses the two-phase error model (upfront Result vs deferred iteration errors) into a single upfront check. This breaks streaming consumption, prevents early termination from saving work, risks violating the memory-cap-50mb parity test on large folder sets, and loses the ability to surface errors that occur mid-iteration (e.g., corrupted records after the first N valid ones).

title: Missing duration parameter
message: The Go interface declares `DelayScan(folder string, next time.Duration)` with a `next` parameter of type `time.Duration` that controls how long to delay the next scan. The Rust implementation `DelayScan(&mut self, folder_id: &str)` omits this parameter entirely, and the delegated call on line 438 likewise passes no duration. Any caller that needs to specify a non-default delay duration cannot do so, breaking semantic equivalence for all non-trivial uses of this method.

title: Return type divergence changes error contract
message: The Go interface method returns nothing (`DelayScan(folder string, next time.Duration)` — no error return), meaning callers cannot observe whether the folder exists via this call's return value. The Rust version returns `Result<(), String>` and explicitly errors with `ErrFolderMissing` on line 437 when the folder is absent. This introduces an error path that does not exist in the Go contract; Go callers and API surfaces that rely on DelayScan being fire-and-forget will behave differently if the Rust side propagates or logs this error, which affects the daemon-api-surface and crash-recovery parity scenarios.

title: Missing 3 enum variants
message: Go defines 7 arms (dbUpdateHandleDir, dbUpdateDeleteDir, dbUpdateHandleFile, dbUpdateDeleteFile, dbUpdateShortcutFile, dbUpdateHandleSymlink, dbUpdateInvalidate) but Rust only defines 5 variants (Update, DeleteFile, DeleteDir, Invalidate, Shortcut), completely omitting equivalents for dbUpdateHandleDir, dbUpdateHandleFile, and dbUpdateHandleSymlink. Any code path that would produce or match these three update types is unrepresentable in the Rust port, breaking folder synchronization for directories, regular files, and symlinks.

title: All string representations differ from Go
message: Every string returned by the Rust method diverges from the Go original: Go returns prefixed names like "dbUpdateDeleteDir", "dbUpdateDeleteFile", "dbUpdateShortcutFile", "dbUpdateHandleInvalidate" while Rust returns shortened forms "delete_dir", "delete_file", "shortcut", "invalidate". If these strings are logged, compared, serialized, or used in any wire/schema context, the mismatch will cause silent incompatibility or matching failures. The Rust variant "Update" maps to "update" which has no single Go counterpart at all.

title: Panic on unknown value not replicated
message: Go panics with a formatted message on any unrecognized dbUpdateType value (line 100), providing a fail-fast diagnostic. Rust uses a closed enum so exhaustive matching prevents this at compile time for known variants, but if the enum is ever extended or received from an external source (e.g., deserialization), the lack of a catch-all error path diverges from Go's defensive behavior. This is lower severity only because Rust's type system partially compensates.

title: Missing error reporting on delete failure
message: Go line 114 calls `f.newScanError(dir, fmt.Errorf("deleting unexpected dir: %w", err))` when `deleteDirOnDisk` returns an error, making the failure observable to the scan-error subsystem. The Rust code at line 2148 checks `.is_ok()` only to increment a counter and silently discards the error, so failed directory deletions are never recorded as scan errors. This loses error observability required by crash-recovery and folder-type-behavior scenarios.

title: Different delete method called
message: Go line 113 calls `f.deleteDirOnDisk(dir, scanChan)`, passing the scan channel into the delete operation so that intermediate scan entries can be emitted during deletion. Rust calls `self.sendReceiveFolder.deleteDirOnDiskHandleChildren(&dir)`, a differently-named method that receives no scan/rescan channel. The "HandleChildren" suffix implies different child-entry semantics, and the absence of a scan channel parameter means any in-deletion scan notifications produced by the Go path are lost in Rust.

title: Return type changed from void to usize
message: Go declares `revertHandleDirs` with no return value (line 100). Rust returns `usize` representing a removed-directory count. This changes the method contract; callers in Go never inspect a return value, so any Rust caller relying on the count has no Go-equivalent behavior, and the count itself only reflects successful deletes rather than total attempts, which could mislead upstream logic.

title: Concurrency model lost around scan processing
message: Go lines 105-107 spawn a concurrent `pullScannerRoutine` goroutine consuming a channel, and each directory is sent to that channel (line 116) so scan processing overlaps with subsequent deletions. Rust replaces this with a synchronous `ScheduleForceRescan` call per directory with no concurrent consumer. If `pullScannerRoutine` performs I/O or batching that depends on receiving items while deletions are still in flight, the Rust port will exhibit different timing and possibly different scan-batch boundaries, affecting crash-recovery correctness.

title: Clone semantics break shared-mutation contract
message: Go returns a slice header referencing the same underlying array of `*Folder` pointers, so callers that mutate a returned Folder element see the change reflected in the owning ClusterConfig. The Rust implementation calls `self.Folders.clone()`, producing a fully independent deep copy; any mutation by the caller is silently discarded and never propagates back to the source ClusterConfig, violating semantic equivalence for any code path that relies on in-place modification of the returned folders (relevant to scenario/protocol-state-transition and scenario/crash-recovery where folder state is updated through returned references).

title: Nil-slice vs empty-Vec distinction collapsed
message: Go distinguishes between a nil Folders field (returns nil, which serializes to JSON `null` and can be detected via `== nil`) and a zero-length initialized slice (returns `[]`, serializable as `[]`). The Rust `.clone()` on an empty `Vec<Folder>` always yields an empty Vec, collapsing this distinction. Any downstream logic or serialization path that branches on nil-vs-empty (e.g., deciding whether ClusterConfig has been populated at all vs explicitly set to no folders) will diverge, which may affect scenario/index-sequence-behavior where unset-vs-empty drives first-sync logic.

title: Owned values instead of pointer-level indirection
message: Go returns `[]*Folder` (slice of pointers), giving callers reference semantics per element; two callers obtaining the same slice share identical `*Folder` pointers. Rust returns `Vec<Folder>` (owned values), so each call site gets independent copies with no aliasing. This affects identity-equality checks and any scenario where two components are expected to observe the same Folder instance (e.g., scenario/memory-cap-50mb, where duplicate pointer-based sharing keeps memory bounded but cloning duplicates the data on every access).

title: Return type and semantic mismatch
message: Go EnumDescriptor returns ([]byte, []int) — the gzipped raw protobuf file descriptor and the enum's index path within that file ([]int{0}). The Rust implementation returns a String formatted as "name:number", which is an entirely different type and carries none of the protobuf reflection metadata. Any consumer relying on the raw descriptor bytes for service registration, reflection, or descriptor pool lookups will receive incompatible data.

title: Receiver arity mismatch
message: Go EnumDescriptor is defined on the type itself with a value receiver and requires no instance state — it returns a file-level constant. The Rust version takes &self and reads self.Number(), making it an instance method whose output varies per variant. The Go method is callable without a concrete enum value; the Rust method is not.

title: Missing raw descriptor and index path
message: The second element of the Go return, []int{0}, encodes the enum's position in the protobuf file descriptor tree and is consumed by protobuf internals (e.g., proto.EnumName, descriptor resolution). The Rust port drops this index entirely and substitutes an unrelated string, breaking wire-level schema introspection and any code path that navigates the descriptor hierarchy.

title: Return type loses structured descriptor
message: The Go method returns `protoreflect.EnumDescriptor`, a rich interface that exposes enum value definitions, full name, options, and parent file descriptor. The Rust port returns `&'static str`, collapsing all structured descriptor access into an opaque string. Any call site that interrogates the descriptor for enum values, source locations, or options will have no equivalent path in Rust, breaking parity for reflection-dependent consumers such as the protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior scenarios.

title: Visibility narrowed to crate-private
message: The Go `Descriptor()` method is exported (uppercase) and callable by any external package. The Rust implementation is declared `pub(crate)`, restricting access to the defining crate only. External crates that depend on `bep-compat` and call `Descriptor()` (e.g., crash-recovery or memory-cap-50mb harnesses) will fail to compile.

title: Error type loses structured error info
message: Go `ListFolders` returns the `error` interface, which supports `errors.Is`/`errors.As` unwrapping and sentinel-error matching (e.g., database-closed or I/O errors). The Rust port uses `Result<Vec<String>, String>`, collapsing all error information into an opaque string. Any caller that needs to branch on error kind (retry on transient failure, ignore on not-found, etc.) cannot do so, breaking behavioral equivalence for error-path handling.

title: Signature and return semantics completely different
message: Go method accepts six parameters (name, stat, item, hasItem, fromDelete, scanChan) and returns an error that callers inspect to distinguish errModified from wrapped CreateFileInfo errors from nil; Rust accepts only a pre-computed bool and returns unit, eliminating all caller-side error discrimination and breaking any upstream code that branches on the error kind.

title: Database and disk comparison logic missing
message: Go calls scanner.CreateFileInfo to build a FileInfo from the on-disk stat and then invokes IsEquivalentOptional with a six-field FileInfoComparison struct (ModTimeWindow, IgnorePerms, IgnoreBlocks=true, IgnoreFlags=LocalAllFlags, IgnoreOwnership conditioned on fromDelete or SyncOwnership, IgnoreXattrs conditioned on fromDelete or SyncXattrs) to detect local modifications; Rust has none of this logic, meaning local file changes that should trigger a rescan are never detected.

title: hasItem and item.Deleted guard missing
message: Go returns errModified when the item is absent from the database (!hasItem) or is marked Deleted, treating a file that appeared from nowhere as modified; Rust has no equivalent branch, so files with no database entry or flagged as deleted will not be recognized as requiring a scan.

title: Deferred scan-channel send replaced with direct ScheduleScan
message: Go uses a deferred closure that sends the file name into scanChan only when the returned error is errModified, allowing batched or asynchronous scan scheduling keyed by filename; Rust unconditionally calls self.folder.ScheduleScan() with no filename context, losing per-file scan granularity and changing the scheduling mechanism.

title: CreateFileInfo error path absent
message: Go wraps and returns errors from scanner.CreateFileInfo (e.g., permission errors reading xattrs) without sending to scanChan, distinguishing transient I/O failures from actual modifications; Rust has no error path, so I/O failures during stat comparison are silently ignored rather than propagated.

title: fromDelete parameter and conditional ownership/xattr ignore missing
message: Go conditions IgnoreOwnership and IgnoreXattrs on the fromDelete flag combined with folder-level SyncOwnership/SyncXattrs settings, meaning delete operations skip ownership and xattr checks; Rust has no fromDelete parameter and no comparison configuration, so delete-triggered scans cannot relax these checks as the Go code requires.

title: Unconditional scan replaces conditional watch-restart
message: The Go implementation guards its side-effect with `if f.FSWatcherEnabled` (lib/model/folder.go:282) and only then calls `scheduleWatchRestart()`. The Rust port removes this guard entirely and unconditionally sets `self.scanScheduled = true`, meaning the method fires even when filesystem watching is disabled. This changes behavior for every folder configuration where FSWatcherEnabled is false: Go does nothing, Rust schedules a scan.

title: Wrong side-effect: scanScheduled instead of scheduleWatchRestart
message: The Go method calls `f.scheduleWatchRestart()` (lib/model/folder.go:283), which restarts the filesystem watcher so that updated ignore patterns are respected by the watcher. The Rust port instead sets `self.scanScheduled = true`, which schedules a full folder scan—a fundamentally different and more expensive operation that does not achieve the same goal of reloading watcher filters. This substitutes watch-restart semantics with scan semantics, breaking behavioral equivalence and likely causing unnecessary I/O under ignore-pattern changes.

title: DeviceID type downgraded to plain string
message: Go `Update` accepts `device protocol.DeviceID`, a 32-byte structured type with checksum validation and a canonical string encoding (Luhn-based). The Rust port declares `device: &str`, losing compile-time type safety and allowing any arbitrary string to be passed without validation. If any implementation compares device identifiers byte-for-byte or uses them as map keys, a non-canonical string representation will silently fail to match, causing silent data loss or phantom device entries. Every other method that accepts `DeviceID` in Go (`drop_all_files`, `drop_device`, `get_device_sequence`, etc.) has the same demotion, but `update` is the most critical because it writes file records keyed by device.

title: Lazy iterator to eager Vec changes memory and error semantics
message: Go iterator-returning methods (e.g., `AllGlobalFiles`) return `(iter.Seq[T], func() error)` providing lazy, streaming evaluation with a deferred error callback. Rust replaces all of these with `Result<Vec<T>, String>`, which eagerly materializes the entire result set into memory before returning. This breaks behavioral equivalence under the `scenario/memory-cap-50mb` parity test because a large folder will allocate unbounded memory in the Rust port, whereas Go streams rows one at a time. It also changes error observability: Go surfaces iteration errors only when the deferred `func() error` is called after iteration completes, while Rust surfaces errors before any element is seen.

title: get_mtime error contract and type diverge from Go
message: Go's `GetMtime` returns `(ondisk, virtual time.Time)` with no error return, meaning it is contractually infallible. Rust's `get_mtime` returns `Result<(i64, i64), String>`, adding a failure path that Go callers never expect. Additionally, `time.Time` carries nanosecond precision, monotonic clock reading, and timezone metadata; replacing it with bare `i64` (presumably epoch nanoseconds) loses timezone and monotonic information, which can cause drift in mtime comparison logic if any consumer relies on `time.Time.Equal()` semantics rather than raw wall-clock comparison.

title: Signed-to-unsigned limit/offset prevents negative sentinels
message: Go declares `limit int` and `offset int` as signed integers in `AllLocalFilesBySequence` and `AllNeededGlobalFiles`. Rust uses `usize` (unsigned). Go implementations commonly accept negative values (e.g., `limit = -1` meaning "no limit") as a sentinel; `usize` makes this impossible, silently changing the semantics of the "unlimited" case and potentially panicking on conversion if a caller attempts to pass a negative value through an `as usize` cast.

title: Local device not excluded from availability
message: Go's SQL query at folderdb_global.go:55 explicitly excludes the local device via `f.device_idx != {{.LocalDeviceIdx}}`. The Rust implementation at db.rs:675 iterates `self.store.devices_in_folder(folder)` which returns all devices including the local device (keyed as "local" per folder_core.rs:23). This means the Rust port will spuriously include the local device in the availability list, causing the caller (model_core.rs:651) to treat the local node as a remote availability source, which breaks pull-source selection and the global-need-decision scenario.

title: No version equivalence check against global winner
message: Go's SQL at folderdb_global.go:53 joins `files g ON g.version_idx = f.version_idx AND g.name_idx = f.name_idx` with the condition `g.local_flags & FlagLocalGlobal != 0`, ensuring only remote devices whose file version matches the current global winner are returned. The Rust implementation at db.rs:676 calls `self.store.get_file(folder, &device, file)` and accepts any non-deleted entry regardless of version. This means devices with stale versions of a file are incorrectly reported as available, and devices holding the global version of a deleted file are incorrectly excluded (due to the `!meta.deleted` filter at db.rs:679 which Go does not apply). This breaks the index-sequence-behavior and global-need-decision parity scenarios.

title: Missing filename normalization
message: Go's implementation at folderdb_global.go:47 calls `osutil.NormalizedFilename(file)` to normalize path separators (e.g., backslash to forward-slash on Windows) before querying the database. The Rust implementation at db.rs:672 passes the `file` parameter directly to the store without any normalization. No equivalent of `NormalizedFilename` exists anywhere in the Rust codebase. On Windows or when callers pass non-normalized paths, lookups will fail to match stored entries, returning empty results where Go would succeed.

title: Wrong data structure mutated
message: The Go interface method `DismissPendingDevice` dismisses a device from the pending/observed-devices store (backed by the database layer `db.ObservedDevice` as shown by `PendingDevices` at lib/model/model.go:125), but the Rust implementation calls `self.connections.remove(device)` which removes the device from the active connections map — an entirely different data structure. This means pending devices are never actually dismissed, and legitimate active connections could be erroneously dropped.

title: Missing error return
message: The Go signature returns `error` (`DismissPendingDevice(device protocol.DeviceID) error`), but the Rust function returns unit `()`. Callers cannot distinguish success from failure (e.g., device not found, database I/O error), breaking the daemon-api-surface parity where HTTP handlers inspect and propagate this error to respond with appropriate status codes.

title: Device parameter type mismatch
message: The Go method accepts `protocol.DeviceID`, a fixed-length 32-byte cryptographic identifier with specific marshaling, comparison, and `String()` formatting semantics. The Rust function accepts `&str`, losing type safety, length validation, and canonical formatting. Any caller passing a non-canonical or malformed string will silently operate on the wrong key rather than failing at the type level.

title: Lazy iterator replaced with eager Vec
message: Go returns `(iter.Seq[FileMetadata], func() error)` — a lazy, streaming iterator plus a deferred error-check closure — while Rust returns `Result<Vec<FileMetadata>, String>`, eagerly collecting all matching rows into memory before returning. This breaks streaming semantics: callers that short-circuit iteration in Go never materialize the full result set, but every Rust caller pays for full materialization.
