This is a direct conflict with the `scenario/memory-cap-50mb` parity test, where large folders with many block-hash matches could exceed the memory budget that the Go version respects via lazy evaluation.

title: Deferred error closure collapsed into single Result
message: Go's second return value `func() error` is a deferred error reporter: callers iterate over results and then call the closure to discover whether the underlying storage encountered an error mid-scan. Rust's `Result<Vec<FileMetadata>, String>` conflates iteration and error reporting into a single call — a storage error during scan either aborts the entire call (returning Err, yielding zero results) or is unreportable (if partial results were already buffered). This changes observable behavior for any caller that processes partial results before checking the error, and means crash-recovery scenarios (`scenario/crash-recovery`) where a read fails mid-iteration will surface differently: Go callers see partial data plus a deferred error, Rust callers see either all data or no data.

title: Return type mismatch: EnumDescriptor vs &str
message: Go returns protoreflect.EnumDescriptor, a rich interface exposing full enum metadata (Values, FullName, Options, ParentFile, etc.) from the compiled proto file descriptor. Rust returns &'static str, a plain string. Any consumer relying on descriptor reflection methods (e.g. iterating enum values, reading options, checking source info) will have no equivalent path in Rust, breaking protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior scenarios that inspect descriptor metadata at runtime.

title: Visibility downgrade from public to pub(crate)
message: Go Descriptor() is exported (public) and callable by any package. Rust declares pub(crate), restricting access to the defining crate only. External crates that need to inspect the enum descriptor for schema negotiation or compatibility checks cannot call this method, breaking cross-crate parity with Go's public API surface.

title: Receiver semantics differ: value receiver vs &self
message: Go defines the method on the bare type `func (MessageType) Descriptor()` meaning the receiver value is unused and the method effectively returns a type-level constant descriptor. Rust takes `&self`, implying an instance is required and could theoretically vary per-value. While functionally equivalent when the body ignores self, the contract difference means Rust callers must construct or hold an instance to obtain the descriptor, unlike Go where a zero-value suffices with no ambiguity about instance dependence.

title: Mapping ID references FileDownloadProgressUpdateType but Go source shows MessageType
message: The review target metadata states the Go symbol is FileDownloadProgressUpdateType.Descriptor, but the actual Go source at lines 70-72 defines the method on MessageType, and it indexes enumTypes[0] which corresponds to MessageType. If a separate FileDownloadProgressUpdateType.Descriptor exists elsewhere (likely indexing a different enumTypes slot), the Rust macro-generated Descriptor may be returning the wrong descriptor string for the wrong enum, causing silent wire-format or schema identity mismatches.

title: Return type loses error channel
message: Go returns `(protocol.FileInfo, bool, error)` — three distinct signals: the file data, a found-boolean, and an error. Rust returns `Option<db::FileInfo>`, collapsing "not found" (`false, nil` in Go) and "error occurred" (`false, err` in Go) into the same `None` variant. Callers that need to distinguish a missing file from a database or folder-lookup error (e.g. the daemon API surface returning 404 vs 500) cannot do so, breaking parity with every call-site that inspects the error return.

title: DB and lock errors silently swallowed
message: `self.sdb.read().ok()?` (line 571) discards a poisoned-lock error, and `.ok()` on `get_global_file` (line 573) discards any database-layer error, both converting them to `None`. In Go, these paths surface a non-nil `error` to the caller. This means the Rust port will silently succeed (return "not found") in scenarios where the Go code propagates actionable errors, violating the crash-recovery and daemon-api-surface parity scenarios.

title: Visibility narrower than Go interface
message: The function is declared `pub(crate)`, restricting it to the current crate. The Go `Model` interface exports `CurrentGlobalFile` as a fully public method available to any importing package. If external crates (e.g. an API or REST handler crate) need to call this method — as the Go REST layer does — they will be unable to, breaking the daemon-api-surface scenario.

title: Return type breaks reflection contract
message: Go returns `protoreflect.Message`, a rich interface supporting descriptor-based field access, mutation (Set/Mutable/NewField), range iteration over populated fields, and WhichOneof queries. The Rust implementation returns a plain `Value` (serialized snapshot) which provides none of these capabilities. Any consumer that calls ProtoReflect to introspect field descriptors, check field presence, or mutate fields via reflection will silently get incorrect behavior or fail to compile. This affects the required parity tests protocol-state-transition and index-sequence-behavior if they rely on reflection-driven dispatch.

title: Nil receiver path missing
message: Go lines 462-469 branch on `x != nil`: when the receiver is nil it falls through to `mi.MessageOf(x)` which returns a valid zero-value reflection object, allowing safe reflection on nil messages. The Rust method takes `&self`, making a None receiver unrepresentable at the call site. If the owning struct is ever held behind an `Option`, callers must add their own None guard, and no equivalent zero-value reflection object is produced for the None case, diverging from Go's guaranteed non-nil return.

title: Lazy message-info initialization absent
message: Go lines 463-466 perform a compare-and-swap lazy init of the message's `MessageInfo` metadata on first reflection access, ensuring the type is registered in the global protobuf registry. The Rust path performs no equivalent registration. Code paths that later query the global type registry (e.g., `protoregistry.GlobalTypes`) to resolve this message type by full name will not find it, which can break dynamic message resolution in crash-recovery or protocol-state-transition scenarios.

title: Missing-global fallthrough instead of skip
message: Go (line 109-113) skips files whose global entry is missing by logging a Failure event and executing `continue`. Rust `Ok(None) => {}` falls through to lines 2061-2063, unconditionally pushing the local file into `updates`. This causes files with no global counterpart to be persisted with their receive-only flag stripped instead of being skipped, corrupting the local index.

title: Missing equivalence check and default-branch version reset
message: Go (lines 132-146) has two distinct branches: an `IsEquivalentOptional` check that adopts the global file when local is equivalent, and a `default` branch that resets `fi.Version` to an empty vector when local genuinely differs from global. Rust collapses both into a single else-branch that always adopts the global file. When local and global are not equivalent, Go produces a record with an empty version vector (strictly older, no conflict), while Rust produces a record with the global's version, breaking conflict-resolution semantics and the folder-type-behavior parity test.

title: Version vector never reset
message: Go resets `fi.Version = protocol.Vector{}` in three places (lines 119, 131, 146) to ensure reverted or deleted files have a strictly-older version that does not conflict. Rust never resets any version vector, so reverted files retain their original versions, defeating the "strictly older" invariant required for correct re-synchronization after revert.

title: No disk deletion of local-only files
message: Go (lines 83-87, 122-131) uses a `deleteQueue` backed by `deleteItemOnDisk`/`deleteDirOnDisk` to physically remove locally-created files from the filesystem before marking them deleted in the database. Rust only sets `local.deleted = true` and clears block hashes without any filesystem deletion, leaving orphaned files on disk that will be re-discovered on the next scan, causing an infinite revert-rescan loop.

title: No directory deletion flush phase
message: Go (lines 157-171) flushes queued directory deletes after the main loop, creating synthetic FileInfo entries (Type=Directory, Deleted=true, Version=empty, ModifiedBy=shortID, ModifiedS=now) for each successfully removed directory. Rust has no equivalent post-loop phase, so locally-created directories are never recorded as deleted in the database and are never removed from disk.

title: Already-deleted file not distinguished in receive-only-global branch
message: Go (line 118-121) checks `if fi.Deleted` before attempting disk deletion when the global is receive-only-changed; if already deleted, it only resets the version vector and breaks to append. Rust unconditionally sets `local.deleted = true` and clears block hashes regardless of prior deletion state, potentially re-deleting or corrupting metadata for files that were already locally deleted.

title: SetDeleted semantics not replicated
message: Go (line 130) calls `fi.SetDeleted(f.shortID)` which sets `ModifiedBy` to the local short ID, sets the `Deleted` flag, clears blocks, and updates modification time. Rust only sets `local.deleted = true` and clears `block_hashes`, omitting `ModifiedBy` assignment, which causes the deleted record to lack provenance and may break cluster-wide delete propagation.

title: No FolderScanning/FolderIdle state transitions
message: Go (lines 76-77) sets the folder state to `FolderScanning` on entry and defers `FolderIdle` on exit. Rust performs no state transitions, so external observers (UI, event subscribers, other goroutines) will not see the folder enter scanning state during revert, breaking status reporting and potentially allowing concurrent operations that Go would serialize.

title: No pullScannerRoutine or scan channel
message: Go (lines 79-81) creates a scan channel and launches `pullScannerRoutine` in a goroutine so that deleted paths are rescanned during revert. Rust has no equivalent, so filesystem changes made during revert are not incrementally scanned, requiring a full scan afterward to reconcile the filesystem with the database.

title: Unbounded Vec violates memory cap
message: Go uses `FileInfoBatch` with `FlushIfFull()` (lines 149-150) to bound memory during revert by flushing to the database in chunks. Rust collects all updates into an unbounded `Vec<FileInfo>` (line 2038), which for large folders may exceed the 50 MB memory cap required by the `memory-cap-50mb` parity test.

title: Rust function is a no-op stub
message: The Rust implementation is an empty stub that only returns `jobs.len()`. It is missing all core behavior: batching with a 1000-item cap and 2-second flush timer, directory fsync logic (lines 1752-1764 in Go), the call to `updateLocalsFromPulling` (line 1769), the `ReceivedFile` notification for file deletions/modifications (lines 1771-1774), the `updateFileInfoChangeTime` call for non-deleted non-invalid files (lines 1804-1816), and the sequence-number reset to zero (line 1817). The function signature also changes the streaming channel-based design (`<-chan dbUpdateJob` with a select loop) to a synchronous slice parameter (`&[dbUpdateJob]`), eliminating the ability to flush partial batches on the 2-second ticker independent of incoming jobs.

title: Missing directory fsync side-effect
message: The Go routine opens each changed parent directory (or the directory itself for `dbUpdateHandleDir` jobs) and calls `Sync()` on it unless `DisableFsync` is set (Go lines 1752-1764). The Rust stub performs no filesystem sync at all, risking data loss on crash, which directly violates the crash-recovery parity test requirement.

title: Missing database commit via updateLocalsFromPulling
message: The Go routine calls `f.updateLocalsFromPulling(files)` on every batch flush (Go line 1769), which persists pulled file metadata to the database. The Rust stub never writes anything to the database, so pulled files will not be recorded and will be re-pulled on every restart.

title: Missing ReceivedFile notification
message: The Go routine calls `f.ReceivedFile(lastFile.Name, lastFile.IsDeleted())` when a handled or deleted file job was seen in the batch (Go lines 1771-1773, guarded by `found`). This notification is absent in Rust, breaking downstream completion tracking.

title: Missing sequence reset to zero
message: The Go routine explicitly sets `job.file.Sequence = 0` (Go line 1817) before appending to the batch, ensuring the database layer assigns a new sequence number. The Rust stub does not reset sequences, which would cause stale or duplicate sequence numbers if the rest of the pipeline were implemented.

title: Missing updateFileInfoChangeTime call
message: For non-deleted, non-invalid files the Go routine calls `f.updateFileInfoChangeTime(&job.file)` (Go lines 1809-1815) to capture the on-disk inode change time. This is absent in Rust, meaning subsequent scans would see a stale change time and unnecessarily re-process files.

title: Signature eliminates streaming and timer-based flush
message: The Go function accepts a channel (`<-chan dbUpdateJob`) and uses a `select` loop with a 2-second ticker to flush partial batches under low throughput (Go lines 1748-1749, 1823-1824). The Rust function accepts a synchronous slice (`&[dbUpdateJob]`), making it impossible to implement the timer-based partial-flush behavior without a redesign of the calling contract.

title: Wrong scan dispatch mechanism
message: The Rust port calls ScheduleForceRescan(&[]) instead of the Go-equivalent DelayScan(0). In Go (folder.go:266-268), DelayScan(0) sends a zero duration into the scanDelay channel, which resets the scan timer to fire immediately for a normal scan. The Rust ScheduleForceRescan (folder_core.rs:515-525) instead inserts an empty-string root entry into self.forcedRescanPaths and calls forcedRescanRequested(), which sets scanScheduled=true. This contaminates the forcedRescanPaths set with a persistent root-level entry that was never intended by the Go code, and the Rust DelayScan method (folder_core.rs:477-478) will early-return if forcedRescanPaths is non-empty, meaning a subsequent normal DelayScan will be silently suppressed. The Go code never modifies forced rescan paths in scanOnWatchErr; it only resets the scan timer.

title: Missing shutdown guard on scan trigger
message: Go's DelayScan(0) (folder.go:267-270) uses a select statement that races the send on f.scanDelay against f.done, silently discarding the scan request if the folder is shutting down. The Rust port unconditionally mutates state (forcedRescanPaths and scanScheduled) with no shutdown check, which could schedule a scan on a folder that is mid-teardown, potentially causing post-shutdown scan execution or state corruption.

title: Entire function body implements wrong logic
message: The Rust function truncates `forcedRescanPaths` to `maxToRemove` entries and returns a count, which has no relation to the Go function's purpose. The Go function performs version cleanup by calling `f.versioner.Clean(ctx)`, managing IO limiter acquisition/release, transitioning through three folder states (FolderCleanWaiting → FolderCleaning → FolderIdle), and resetting the cleanup timer. None of these operations exist in the Rust implementation; the entire body appears to be copied from a different function.

title: Missing state machine transitions
message: The Go function sets state to `FolderCleanWaiting` on entry, transitions to `FolderCleaning` after acquiring the IO limiter, and defers a reset to `FolderIdle` on exit. The Rust function performs no state transitions at all, which breaks any observer or UI that depends on folder state to report cleanup progress.

title: Missing IO limiter acquire and release
message: The Go function acquires an IO limiter token via `f.ioLimiter.TakeWithContext(ctx, 1)` before performing work and defers `f.ioLimiter.Give(1)` to release it. This throttles concurrent IO-heavy operations. The Rust function has no limiter interaction, so it cannot participate in IO backpressure and may allow unbounded concurrent IO when other folder operations also expect limiter coordination.

title: Missing versioner.Clean call
message: The core side effect of the Go function—invoking `f.versioner.Clean(ctx)` to purge old file versions—is entirely absent from the Rust implementation. Without this call, old versioned files will accumulate indefinitely, violating the version-cleanup behavioral contract.

title: Missing timer reset
message: The Go function resets `f.versionCleanupTimer` with `f.versionCleanupInterval` at line 1049 to schedule the next periodic cleanup. The Rust function does not reset any timer, so periodic version cleanup will fire at most once and never recur.

title: Wrong signature: return type and missing context
message: The Go function signature is `func (f *folder) versionCleanupTimerFired(ctx context.Context)` returning nothing, while the Rust function is `fn versionCleanupTimerFired(&mut self) -> usize` returning a count and accepting no cancellation context. The return type mismatch will propagate to callers, and the missing context means the function cannot support cooperative cancellation on the IO limiter acquire path.

title: Missing rename detection in scan loop
message: Go lines 744-756 call findRename for folder types other than ReceiveOnly and ReceiveEncrypted, performing a second batch.Update and incrementing the changes counter when a rename is detected. The Rust scan_filesystem_deterministic performs no rename detection whatsoever during the filesystem walk; the findRename method exists at line 564 but is never invoked during scanning, causing renamed files to be treated as a delete plus an add instead of a single rename operation, which changes both the changes count and the DB update sequence emitted to peers.

title: Missing folder-type branch for hashing vs no-hashing
message: Go lines 710-714 branch on FolderTypeReceiveEncrypted to call scanner.WalkWithoutHashing instead of scanner.Walk, avoiding expensive hash computation for encrypted folders. Rust always calls hash_file_blocks at line 1410 regardless of folder type; there is no concept of WalkWithoutHashing, meaning receive-encrypted folders will be incorrectly hashed and will produce different FileInfo block_hashes values than Go peers, breaking wire compatibility for encrypted folder sync.

title: Missing ignore/matcher pattern integration
message: Go passes f.ignores as scanConfig.Matcher (line 691) so the scanner skips files matching user-configured ignore patterns (.stignore). Rust's scan_filesystem_deterministic does not consult any ignore patterns during the walk or file processing; should_skip_scan_path (line 1340) only checks hardcoded prefixes like .stfolder and .DS_Store. Files that should be ignored per .stignore will be indexed and synced, breaking the conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity test.

title: Return type drops streaming cfiler and already-used map
message: Go returns (int, *streamingCFiler, map[string]struct{}, error) where the streamingCFiler carries collected deleted files for Phase 2 delete processing and the map tracks files already used for rename detection. Rust returns Result<ScanExecutionStats, String> with no cfiler or tracking map. The caller in Go relies on the returned cfiler to process Phase 2 deletions; without it, the Rust call-site must handle delete detection entirely within scan_filesystem_deterministic (lines 1080-1091), but this collapses the two-phase architecture and changes how delete batching interacts with the scan batch flush logic.

title: Per-file errors halt entire scan instead of continuing
message: Go lines 722-725 handle per-file scan errors by calling newScanError and continuing to the next file, allowing the scan to complete with partial errors recorded. Rust's process_scanned_file calls fs::symlink_metadata at line 1387 and returns Err on failure, which propagates through process_path (line 1050) to set scan_error, halting the entire scan. This means a single inaccessible file aborts the scan of the entire folder, which differs from Go behavior and will fail crash-recovery scenarios where partial results are expected.

title: Missing context cancellation and channel drain on flush error
message: Go lines 727-733 on FlushIfFull error explicitly cancel the scan context and drain the channel before returning, preventing goroutine leaks and ensuring the scanner walk terminates cleanly. Rust's flush_scan_updates_if_full at line 1053 on error sets scan_error but the walk continues processing paths until the closure checks scan_error at line 1034; there is no mechanism to interrupt walk_deterministic mid-traversal, so the walk completes fully before the error is observed, wasting resources and potentially producing side effects on an aborted scan.

title: Missing scanner config fields for permissions, xattrs, normalization, and progress
message: Go scanner.Config (lines 688-706) includes IgnorePerms, AutoNormalize, Hashers count, ShortID, ProgressTickIntervalS, LocalFlags, ModTimeWindow, EventLogger, ScanOwnership, ScanXattrs, XattrFilter, and TempLifetime. None of these have equivalents in the Rust implementation. While some may be deferred features, LocalFlags (line 700) and ModTimeWindow (line 701) directly affect whether a file is considered changed, meaning the Rust scan may produce different update decisions than Go for the same filesystem state.
